EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After improving during June 2007, the long-term drought across much of northeast Minnesota worsened during July 2007, especially over portions of Cook, Lake, and St. Louis counties, where severe (D2) drought conditions existed by month's end. Total monthly rainfall was below normal, with an average of just 1 to 2 inches. Runoff, river and stream flow were also below normal across the region.

An upper jet over the northern plains helped to enhance snow development with this system. At the surface, a weak area of low pressure tracked from north central Nebraska into southern Wisconsin, while an inverted trough extended up to the north. This brought about a 12 hour period of heavy snow, followed by gusty north northwest winds (on the 3rd). Numerous schools and activities closed across the area on Tuesday (3rd). Many vehicles were reported in the ditch, especially along Interstate 94 between Moorhead and Fergus Falls. 10 inches of snow was reported at Norcross, 9 inches near Battle Lake, and 7 inches at Ponsford, Wadena, and Wendell.

Late in the evening of the 17th, an area of surface low pressure was located just northeast of Jamestown, North Dakota, with a cold front extending south from the low. A warm front arched to the northeast, from near Jamestown to Grand Forks to Bemidji (MN). From the surface through the low layers of the atmosphere, wind speeds were quite strong. In the late afternoon of the 17th, one lone thunderstorm cell developed over Polk County (MN). By early evening, another lone thunderstorm developed over Ramsey County (ND). Finally, just before midnight, a line of thunderstorms took shape over eastern North Dakota. This line of storms moved into northwest Minnesota around midnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seven empty railroad grain cars were tipped off a railroad track. 62 other empty railroad cars were pushed one mile south of their parked location.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many large tree branches were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow, producing heavy accumulations, was accompanied by winds gusting over 40 mph. The snow began early on March 1st after a brief respite from the previous snowfall, with winds increasing as the day went on. Blizzard conditions with widespread near zero visibility in snow and blowing snow resulted. Roads were blocked by drifting and travel was made impossible or extremely difficult. Interstate 90 was closed during the storm. Numerous businesses and schools were forced to close, and school athletic events and other activities were cancelled. Of those who did attempt to travel during the storm, several vehicles were stranded and others slid off roads. There were several power outages. The weight of the heavy snow and the winds caused several machine shed roofs to collapse in the Tyler to Marshall area, with some machinery damage resulting. The blizzard followed another significant snow 1 to 2 days before. The snow decreased on the night of March 2nd, with blizzard conditions abating by early morning on March 3rd. New snow accumulations ranged from 8 to 16 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: New snowfall of 8 to 12 inches beginning early morning on March 1st and continuing into the night of March 2nd was accompanied by sustained winds of over 30 mph at times with gusts over 40 mph. The combination of new snow, wind, and an already existing fresh snow cover resulted in a blizzard with widespread near zero visibilities. Drifting snow with the storm helped make travel impossible to extremely difficult. As a result, some who did attempt to travel became stuck or slid off roads. Schools and school activities were cancelled and numerous businesses closed. A few power outages were reported as the heavy snow and strong winds brought down power lines. The blizzard conditions abated early on the morning of March 3rd.|

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain, which caused flash flooding, in Nobles and Jackson Counties in southwest Minnesota during the morning of August 20th. The flash flooding affected roads in both rural and urban areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall of over 3 inches in a short time caused flooding of numerous roads in central and western Jackson County. Roads flooded included Minnesota Highway 86 just south of Interstate 90, several roads in the town of Okabena, and many rural roads. Fields in the area were also flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lack of substantial rainfall into early June kept Lake of the Woods and northern Beltrami counties in a D2 Drought Designation (per the U.S. Drought Monitor).

By noon on the 7th, a surface low was located near Lake Park, Minnesota, with a trailing cold front extending back into extreme southeast North Dakota. An occluded front also extended to the northeast, toward the Bemidji, MN, area. Surface dew points pooled right around 60 F in the vicinity of the surface low with temperatures in the middle 70s. The cold front was strong for early June, with a noon temperature in Devils Lake, ND, of 48 degrees. This set up a strong surface thermal and moisture boundary along a Wahpeton-Breckenridge to Bemidji line. Storms initially fired across southeast ND and then spread into portions of west central and northwest MN. There was also strong upper level support for these storms, as one severe report north of Embden (in Cass County, ND) occurred well behind the surface boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree (about 3 feet in diameter) was uprooted, which damaged the roof of a nearby house.

Late in the afternoon of the 9th, a cold front was located just to the north of the Canadian border (across southern Saskatchewan, southern Manitoba, and southwestern Ontario). Temperatures at 500 millibars were quite cold. As temperatures warmed at the surface and an upper level disturbance moved through, thunderstorms were able to form and intensify enough to produce hail.

Around 3 pm CST, a fairly strong cold front was located along a line from Devils Lake, ND, to Hallock, MN. Throughout the evening, this cold front slowly sagged southward. By 6 pm CST, the front had reached a line from New Rockford, ND, to Roseau, MN. At this time, Jamestown, ND, reported a temperature of 86 degrees, while Devils Lake had dropped to 66 degrees. By midnight, the front was located along a line from Bemidji (MN) to Fargo (ND) to 20 miles south of Jamestown (ND). Behind the front, the temperature dropped to 39 degrees in Devils Lake a couple hours after midnight. Large hail, tornadoes, and flash flooding occurred during this event.

Rainfall was persistent across southeast North Dakota and portions of west central Minnesota throughout the month of May. NWS Cooperative Observers reported May totals of 7.52 inches at Wahpeton/Breckenridge and 7.44 inches at Rothsay. These values were over 200 percent of normal. From June 1st into the 2nd, a nearly stacked low pressure system tracked across South Dakota into southwest Minnesota. Showers and thunderstorms pivoted around the low as it tracked to the east, bringing substantial rainfall amounts to extreme southeast North Dakota and portions of west central Minnesota. Rainfall totals from these two days amounted to 4.73 inches at Wahpeton/Breckenridge and 1.97 inches at Rothsay.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many rural roads were flooded by the heavy rain.

A surface low tracked from southwest Minnesota into northern Wisconsin, while an inverted trough set up across northwest Minnesota. This brought about a 12 hour period of heavy snow in a band from Roseau to Red Lake Falls, where 6 to 6.5 inches of snow was reported.

A stationary front had been located along a Roseau (Minnesota) to Grand Forks (North Dakota) line around midday. This boundary essentially washed out by late afternoon. However, showers and thunderstorms continued along the boundary for most of the day, and one stronger cell formed by late afternoon. This thunderstorm cell produced the tornado and funnel cloud reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple funnels were seen over Clearwater Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced large hail near the town of Bigelow in Nobles County in southwest Minnesota on the evening of August 20th.

Snow, producing heavy accumulations, was accompanied by winds gusting over 40 mph. The snow began early on March 1st after a brief respite from the previous snowfall, with winds increasing as the day went on. Blizzard conditions with widespread near zero visibility in snow and blowing snow resulted. Roads were blocked by drifting and travel was made impossible or extremely difficult. Interstate 90 was closed during the storm. Numerous businesses and schools were forced to close, and school athletic events and other activities were cancelled. Of those who did attempt to travel during the storm, several vehicles were stranded and others slid off roads. There were several power outages. The weight of the heavy snow and the winds caused several machine shed roofs to collapse in the Tyler to Marshall area, with some machinery damage resulting. The blizzard followed another significant snow 1 to 2 days before. The snow decreased on the night of March 2nd, with blizzard conditions abating by early morning on March 3rd. New snow accumulations ranged from 8 to 16 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: New snowfall of 10 to 16 inches beginning early morning on March 1st and continuing into the night of March 2nd was accompanied by sustained winds of over 30 mph at times with gusts over 40 mph. The combination of new snow, wind, and an already existing fresh snow cover resulted in a blizzard with widespread near zero visibilities. Drifting snow with the storm helped make travel impossible to extremely difficult. As a result, some who did attempt to travel became stuck or slid off roads. Schools and school activities were cancelled and numerous businesses closed. Power outages were reported as the heavy snow and strong winds brought down power lines. The blizzard conditions abated early on the morning of March 3rd.|

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late in the evening of the 17th, an area of surface low pressure was located just northeast of Jamestown, North Dakota, with a cold front extending south from the low. A warm front arched to the northeast, from near Jamestown to Grand Forks to Bemidji (MN). From the surface through the low layers of the atmosphere, wind speeds were quite strong. In the late afternoon of the 17th, one lone thunderstorm cell developed over Polk County (MN). By early evening, another lone thunderstorm developed over Ramsey County (ND). Finally, just before midnight, a line of thunderstorms took shape over eastern North Dakota. This line of storms moved into northwest Minnesota around midnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many large trees were snapped off or uprooted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper jet over the northern plains helped to enhance snow development with this system. At the surface, a weak area of low pressure tracked from north central Nebraska into southern Wisconsin, while an inverted trough extended up to the north. This brought about a 12 hour period of heavy snow, followed by gusty north northwest winds (on the 3rd). Numerous schools and activities closed across the area on Tuesday (3rd). Many vehicles were reported in the ditch, especially along Interstate 94 between Moorhead and Fergus Falls. 10 inches of snow was reported at Norcross, 9 inches near Battle Lake, and 7 inches at Ponsford, Wadena, and Wendell.

Late in the afternoon of the 9th, a cold front was located just to the north of the Canadian border (across southern Saskatchewan, southern Manitoba, and southwestern Ontario). Temperatures at 500 millibars were quite cold. As temperatures warmed at the surface and an upper level disturbance moved through, thunderstorms were able to form and intensify enough to produce hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail fell near the Polk and Red Lake County line.

Around 3 pm CST, a fairly strong cold front was located along a line from Devils Lake, ND, to Hallock, MN. Throughout the evening, this cold front slowly sagged southward. By 6 pm CST, the front had reached a line from New Rockford, ND, to Roseau, MN. At this time, Jamestown, ND, reported a temperature of 86 degrees, while Devils Lake had dropped to 66 degrees. By midnight, the front was located along a line from Bemidji (MN) to Fargo (ND) to 20 miles south of Jamestown (ND). Behind the front, the temperature dropped to 39 degrees in Devils Lake a couple hours after midnight. Large hail, tornadoes, and flash flooding occurred during this event.

By noon on the 7th, a surface low was located near Lake Park, Minnesota, with a trailing cold front extending back into extreme southeast North Dakota. An occluded front also extended to the northeast, toward the Bemidji, MN, area. Surface dew points pooled right around 60 F in the vicinity of the surface low with temperatures in the middle 70s. The cold front was strong for early June, with a noon temperature in Devils Lake, ND, of 48 degrees. This set up a strong surface thermal and moisture boundary along a Wahpeton-Breckenridge to Bemidji line. Storms initially fired across southeast ND and then spread into portions of west central and northwest MN. There was also strong upper level support for these storms, as one severe report north of Embden (in Cass County, ND) occurred well behind the surface boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters at the Indian Health Service observed a garbage bag spiral into the updraft area of the storm, but no touchdown or damage occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread snow developed quickly over southwest Minnesota on the early morning of December 1st, and continued into the afternoon and early evening of the same day. The snow was heavy north of Interstate 90 with up to 7 inches of accumulation. Freezing rain accompanied the snow for part of the storm near Interstate 90.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 5 to 7 inches over the county and contributed to several vehicle accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moving northeast into the northern plains from New Mexico helped produce moderate snow accumulations over a large area, including southwest Minnesota. The snow fell during the early morning hours of January 21st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 3 to 5 inches slowed travel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread freezing rain developed on February 24th and produced ice accumulations of near or greater than a quarter inch. The freezing rain changed to snow by the night of the 24th, then moderate to heavy snow continued through the morning and into the early afternoon of February 25th. Snow accumulations ranged from 5 to 11 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread freezing rain produced ice accumulations near or above a quarter inch on February 24th. The icing made travel extremely difficult. The precipitation changed to snow late on the 24th, with snow accumulating 7 to 11 inches before ending by early afternoon on February 25th. The snow accumulations on top of the ice made travel impossible in places. The combination also caused tree branches and a few power lines to break, producing scattered power outages. Some weekend school activities were cancelled, as were several Sunday church services.

A lack of rainfall over the summer and fall of 2006, and a lack of snowfall through the early winter of 2006-07, resulted in several counties across northwest Minnesota being put in a D2 (severe) drought designation (per the U.S. Drought Monitor). Kittson and West Marshall counties were added to the D2 category on February 13, and remained there for the rest of the month.

Surface high pressure built down across the western high plains with a very cold airmass associated with it. By the morning of the 12th, temperatures fell to -18F to -28F across northeast North Dakota and the extreme northwest corner of Minnesota. Just enough of a surface pressure gradient remained over the area to keep northwest winds at 10 to 15 mph.  This combination of very cold air and steady northwest wind speeds kept wind chill readings from -40F to -50F through the morning hours of the 12th.

Snow, primarily associated with low pressure aloft, produced heavy accumulations over part of southwest Minnesota including the Worthington, Windom, and Jackson areas. Lesser accumulations were reported elsewhere in southwest Minnesota in a band just to the northwest of this area. The snow fell from the afternoon of January 14th into the predawn hours of January 15th. Increasing northwest winds and falling temperatures caused dangerous wind chills near the end of the event. The winds also caused a few areas of blowing and drifting snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulated 5 to 7 inches. Increasing northwest winds during the event lowered wind chills to 20 below zero before the snow ended. The winds also caused a few areas of blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extended period of cold weather and wind created dangerously low wind chill values across much of southeast Minnesota with values reaching as low as 41 below in Mower County (Dexter), 40 below in Olmsted County (Rochester), and 37 below in Dodge County (Dodge Center).  It was the coldeset start to a February since 1996.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill values dropped to -41 deg F at Dexter on the morning of February 3rd.  It hit -37 at Austin as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low amounts of precipitation over the course of March 2007 kept many counties across northwest and west central Minnesota in a D2 (severe) or D3 (extreme) drought designation (per the U.S. Drought Monitor). Although there were three distinct precipitation events in March (early, mid, and late month), this was not enough moisture to end the drought designation placed on most counties. Roseau, Lake of the Woods, North and South Beltrami, North Clearwater, and Hubbard counties remained in the D3 designation all month. East Marshall, Pennington, South Clearwater, and Wadena counties began the month in a D3 designation, and then dropped down to a D2 designation on March 6th, where they remained through the rest of the month. Red Lake, East Polk, Mahnomen, and East Becker counties remained in a D2 designation all month. Kittson and West Marshall counties began the month in a D2 designation, but were dropped from the D2 designation on March 6.

An area of surface low pressure tracked from near Watford City, ND, around midnight on the 15th, to Wadena, MN, on the afternoon of the 15th. A fairly compact upper level disturbance also accompanied the surface low, helping to produce a fairly narrow band of heavy snow. The heavy snow band fell from Cando to Manvel, ND, on toward Thief River Falls, MN. Snow amounts ranging from 6 to 8 inches were common along this roughly 50 mile wide band.

An approaching cold front triggered a line of severe thunderstorms during the late afternoon of September 21. Storm spotters, law enforcement officials, amateur radio operators and the public reported wind gusts around 60 mph. As a result, trees and power lines were knocked down.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds also occurred, but no speed was given.

Wind speeds increased from the evening of the 1st into the evening of the 2nd, causing whiteout conditions from Moorhead to Fergus Falls. No travel was advised, due to zero visibility and snow drifts on roads. Many snow plows were pulled, as they could not keep up with the blowing and drifting snow.

Kittson and West Marshall counties began the month in a D2 designation, but were dropped from the D2 designation on March 6.

By noon CST on the 27th, a weak area of surface low pressure was located over southeast South Dakota, with a stationary front extending to its north-northeast (along a Lidgerwood, ND, to Waskish, MN, line). Thunderstorms initially formed along a line from south of Bismarck (ND) to near Bemidji (MN), and across the tri-state boundary of ND, SD, and MN. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms over central North Dakota flared up late in the morning of the 10th, and then tracked into northeast North Dakota and northwest Minnesota through the day. These storms initially formed near a stationary front, which stretched from Winnipeg, Manitoba, Canada, to near Bismarck, ND. By noon CST, the front had progressed to a Langdon (ND) to Devils Lake (ND) line. The airmass ahead of the front was hot and humid (at noon CST), with Jamestown (ND) at 86F and Crookston (MN) at 88F. During the early afternoon, an outflow boundary formed over western Grand Forks County and tracked to the east, eventually reaching a Roseau (MN) to Crookston (MN) to Jamestown (ND) line by late afternoon. These storms also had rich moisture up through 850mb and support from the low level jet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several tree branches were broken off and some tin roofing was blown off a shed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of the 13th, one large supercell tracked across southwest Benson County (ND) and western Eddy County (ND). This storm tracked southeast toward northern Barnes County (ND), then weakened as it moved into Cass County (ND). A second large supercell then developed in Towner County (ND) and tracked toward Thompson, ND. As the second storm moved into southeast Grand Forks County (ND), additional storms developed to the east along and just south of the U.S. Highway 2 corridor in Minnesota. As the second supercell moved across eastern Ramsey and northern Nelson Counties (in ND), it displayed a classic hook echo on radar.

In the early evening of the 9th, a large thunderstorm complex was located near Dauphin, Manitoba, Canada. This complex was near the crest of a mid level ridge, which extended from southwest Manitoba all the way down into the southern plains. As these storms passed away from the crest of the ridge, they took on an east-southeast movement. This took the thunderstorm complex right through the Winnipeg, Manitoba, area (just after midnight). During its track across southern Manitoba, this storm complex produced hail up to softball size along with very strong winds. As the Manitoba storm complex continued on its east-southeast track, it accelerated to around 45 knots as it approached the northern Lake of the Woods communities and resorts. The complex struck the Angle Inlet, Flag Island, and Oak Island areas around 3 am CST, then continued across extreme southwest Ontario and on through the International Falls, MN, area by early morning (10th).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A house shook and the roof flexed as the strong outflow winds hit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: By late afternoon of the 13th, a prefrontal trough was located from northwest Roseau County (MN) to Crookston (MN) to Oakes (ND), while an upstream cold front approached the North Dakota/Canada border. By early evening, the cold front had quickly pushed down to a Minot (ND) to Hillsboro (ND) to Mahnomen (MN) line. Several thunderstorms formed near these surface boundaries, but did not last long. Model soundings showed an Inverted-V profile, which is favorable for stronger winds aloft to mix down to the surface. Therefore, the wind events were likely associated with dying thunderstorm cells.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind gust was measured by a MNDOT RWIS sensor along U.S. Highway 2 west of Fisher.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms near Winnipeg moved southeast and clipped the Lake of the Woods region on the morning of the 22nd. These thunderstorms had deep moisture to work with, as 850mb dew points ranged from 18C to 20C, and surface dew points were around 70F. These storms were elevated (not surface based) and were fueled by the low level jet and good warm air advection.

Dry conditions which existed at the start of July worsened during the month with little rain falling. The drought index reached the severe drought category during the month, eventually leading to farmers in 6 southwest Minnesota counties being made eligible for federal drought assistance. One of the counties, Pipestone, was named a primary disaster area for the drought.||Crops such as corn and soybeans, as well as pasture land and general forage were deemed hurt by the drought. In addition to the crop losses, the pasture and forage losses led to difficulties in livestock operations. The amount of the losses could not readily be assessed before harvest, but were suspected to be substantial.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dry conditions existing at the start of July combined with little rain falling during the month to worsen drought into the severe category. By the end of July, corn crops were rated in condiiitions ranging from fair to very poor, with substantial damaged deemed certain even if rains followed immediately. Soybean and other crops, as well as pasture and forage suffered as well. The pasture and forage losses led to difficulties in livestock operations, leading to additional expenses required to keep livestock fed. The amount of the losses could not readily be assessed before harvest, but were suspected to be substantial. The drought condiitions eventually led to the county being declared a federal disaster area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Around noon CST, a weak surface trough was located from Mahnomen County back into southeast North Dakota. Over the next several hours, the trough did not move much. By early afternoon, a weak surface low formed over central Minnesota, with a cold front extending back into southwest MN. A few thunderstorms flared up in the early afternoon near the trough over Mahnomen and Otter Tail Counties, but these storms did not last long.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind gust was measured by a MNDOT RWIS sensor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: There was very little surface support for this event, with light surface winds and no frontal boundaries. However, a decent upper level jet did accompany this event. Thunderstorms in southeast Manitoba (Canada) moved southeast and clipped a portion of the Lake of the Woods region in the early evening. About an hour later, another group of thunderstorms over southwest/southern Manitoba moved across the border into northeast North Dakota and a portion of northwest Minnesota. These thunderstorms weakened by late evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail and very strong wind occurred just south of Driftwood Point.

By late afternoon of the 13th, a prefrontal trough was located from northwest Roseau County (MN) to Crookston (MN) to Oakes (ND), while an upstream cold front approached the North Dakota/Canada border. By early evening, the cold front had quickly pushed down to a Minot (ND) to Hillsboro (ND) to Mahnomen (MN) line. Several thunderstorms formed near these surface boundaries, but did not last long. Model soundings showed an Inverted-V profile, which is favorable for stronger winds aloft to mix down to the surface. Therefore, the wind events were likely associated with dying thunderstorm cells.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dozens of trees were damaged on the northwest shore of Lake Lizzie. Some of the trees were snapped in half. Several houses and cars were damaged from falling trees. The wind lasted less than 5 minutes and occurred after rain had started. An observation from a witness suggested this was a wet microburst.

Rain changed to wet snow on April 10th. The snow continued into the early morning of April 11th, producing accumulatiuons of 5 to 8 inches across southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wey snow accumulated 5 to 8 inches, causing slippery roads and a few accidents. The snow forced the cancellation of several mainly outdoor activities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This event set up as another nice case of elevated convection. On the morning of the 20th, an area of surface low pressure was located near Mobridge, South Dakota, with a warm front arching to the southeast (down into eastern Nebraska). A 35 to 45 knot low level jet set up from central Nebraska into northeast South Dakota, where there was also a tight moisture gradient at 850 mb. Thunderstorms initially flared up near Crookston, MN, and moved northeast at 40 to 50 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ground was covered with hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front, upper level shortwave trough, and lake breeze set off thunderstorms on the afternoon of May 14th.  Widespread large hail fell from northwest of Duluth in the Twig, Caribou Lake and Pike Lake areas and in Duluth, east of the Piedmont Heights neighborhood. The hail dented cars and damaged roofs of houses. Total damage was roughly estimated at $700,000.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One to 2 inch diameter hail fell in Lakewood Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front and unstable air mass combined to produce severe thunderstorms across southern Cass, Crow Wing, and Pine counties during the evening hours of August 13. A fast-moving severe thunderstorm in the Brainerd area brought large hail and winds up to 60 mph that downed hundreds of trees and damaged homes. Several businesses reported roof damage and a two-story structure at a local speedway collapsed. Docks along several lakes in Crow Wing County also reported damage. At least 7,700 customers lost power in the Brainerd Lakes region, according to Crow Wing Power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two 18-inch diameter trees were blown down by the strong thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced damaging winds in southwest Minnesota near Jasper around midnight on the night of May 22nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused tree damage, including numerous branches blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm moved from the southern plains into the Ohio Valley, producing widespread precipitation across the Midwest. In southeast Minnesota, the storm was the first of the winter season, producing a mix of heavy snow and sleet much of Saturday December 1. Precipitation changed to mainly freezing drizzle or freezing rain during the evening hours, before ending in the early morning of Sunday December 2. Unusually high accumulations of sleet were measured by weather observers, with amounts between 1 and 2 inches. Specific reports included 1.9 inches near Reno (Houston County) and 1 inch near Grand Meadow (Mower County). Total snow and sleet accumulations generally ranged from 3 to 6 inches, with 6 inches reported near Spring Valley (Fillmore County) and 5.5 inches near Houston (Houston County). There was some freezing rain during the event, with ice accumulations less than two tenths of an inch.

A weak cold front passed into northeast North Dakota and extreme northwest Minnesota during the evening of the 23rd. A strong low level jet was located along and just to the east of the surface cold front, where 50 to 55 knots was measured in the 850-925 mb layer. Warm mid level temperatures kept most convection at bay, but in a thin area right along the boundary (from northwest of Gilby to east of Grafton, ND, to near Lancaster, MN), some weak thunderstorms mixed some of these stronger wind gusts down to the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One large poplar tree was knocked down.

An unseasonably strong surface low moved out of the Dakotas, producing severe weather across the upper midwest. The first round of severe storms moved through eastern Itasca and northwest St. Louis counties around dawn. More widespread severe thunderstorms developed in the afternoon and continued into early evening, producing large hail and damaging winds. A gust front ahead of a line of storms produced up to 70 mph winds from Itasca County through southern St. Louis County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A twelve inch diameter tree was blown down on a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through northeast Minnesota during the afternoon hours of July 26. A line of thunderstorms developed and became severe, producing damaging winds and some hail. Tree limbs were downed in the Duluth area as the storms passed through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The severe thunderstorm winds occurred at the intersection of Interstate 35 and Highway 210.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small cluster of thunderstorms near International Falls converged into a lone supercell over Koochiching County. The storm moved south into Itasca County, producing damaging straight line winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A twelve-inch inch diameter tree was blown down across Highway 48. Power outages from falling trees and limbs were reported county-wide.

At least 7,700 customers lost power in the Brainerd Lakes region, according to Crow Wing Power.

A cold front, upper level shortwave trough, and lake breeze set off thunderstorms on the afternoon of May 14th.  Widespread large hail fell from northwest of Duluth in the Twig, Caribou Lake and Pike Lake areas and in Duluth, east of the Piedmont Heights neighborhood. The hail dented cars and damaged roofs of houses. Total damage was roughly estimated at $700,000.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail fell at the Kenwood Shopping Center.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front and unstable air mass contributed to very intense thunderstorm development in northeast Minnesota during the afternoon and evening hours of August 27. One particularly severe thunderstorm produced widespread hail damage in the Big Sandy Lake area of Aitkin County, damaging cars, boats, roofs and sidings of homes. A separate thunderstorm produced a lightning strike that caused extensive damage to Queen of Peace Catholic Church in Cloquet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail caused minor crop damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm moved from the southern plains into the Ohio Valley, producing widespread precipitation across the Midwest. In southeast Minnesota, the storm was the first of the winter season, producing a mix of heavy snow and sleet much of Saturday December 1. Precipitation changed to mainly freezing drizzle or freezing rain during the evening hours, before ending in the early morning of Sunday December 2. Unusually high accumulations of sleet were measured by weather observers, with amounts between 1 and 2 inches. Specific reports included 1.9 inches near Reno (Houston County) and 1 inch near Grand Meadow (Mower County). Total snow and sleet accumulations generally ranged from 3 to 6 inches, with 6 inches reported near Spring Valley (Fillmore County) and 5.5 inches near Houston (Houston County). There was some freezing rain during the event, with ice accumulations less than two tenths of an inch.

An unseasonably strong surface low moved out of the Dakotas, producing severe weather across the upper midwest. The first round of severe storms moved through eastern Itasca and northwest St. Louis counties around dawn. More widespread severe thunderstorms developed in the afternoon and continued into early evening, producing large hail and damaging winds. A gust front ahead of a line of storms produced up to 70 mph winds from Itasca County through southern St. Louis County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and powerlines were blown down by the strong thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved south through southern Canada and crossed the border into northern Minnesota during the afternoon of July 11. One particular storm became severe and produced downburst winds that caused damage in International Falls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds blew decks from homes and blew down many pine trees. One-quarter of a garage roof was blown off.

Rapid snowmelt runoff from 25 to 30 inches of snow and ice jamming on the Little Minnesota River and Lake Traverse resulted in flash flooding in Browns Valley. The water came mostly into the northern end of town around 1 to 2 am in the morning of the 14th. One-hundred forty homes received some flooding damage with fifty homes receiving significant damage. Over 100 residents had to be evacuated. Several roads and bridges also received damage from the flooding. The flood waters slowly receded through the early morning hours of the 17th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Just after midnight CST on the 7th, a weak area of surface low pressure was located near the Northwest Angle of Minnesota. A cold front stretched southward from the low, along a line from Warroad to Detroit Lakes (MN). By 3 am CST, the front had moved east of Lake of the Woods and Beltrami Counties. In the 850 mb layer, there was a tight north-south moisture and thermal gradient over and behind the surface front along with about a 35 knot low level jet. Thunderstorms formed mainly in this elevated layer and produced some large hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This event set up as another nice case of elevated convection. On the morning of the 20th, an area of surface low pressure was located near Mobridge, South Dakota, with a warm front arching to the southeast (down into eastern Nebraska). A 35 to 45 knot low level jet set up from central Nebraska into northeast South Dakota, where there was also a tight moisture gradient at 850 mb. Thunderstorms initially flared up near Crookston, MN, and moved northeast at 40 to 50 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck an evergreen tree in Barrett, scattering pieces of the tree across several neighboring yards. As the tree fell, it broke multiple windows and damaged the concrete steps to a house. The strike also damaged the electrical systems in three nearby homes. One home lost a microwave, two televisions, and several other smaller appliances.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a cold front moved into southeast Minnesota during the afternoon of June 7, it triggered a cluster of severe thunderstorms. Most storm damage was in areas south of a line from Winona to Preston. Law enforcement officials, amateur radio operators and storm spotters reported wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph, which knocked down trees. A shed and garage were also damaged near Rushford (Fillmore County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twelve to eighteen inch trees were blown down from strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A man died of a lightning strike on Bowstring Lake on the evening of June 8th. He may have been on his dock when struck. His body was found in the lake the following day.

An intense area of low pressure brought heavy rains to portions of northeast Minnesota on October 18 through the morning hours of October 19. Rainfall amounts of 2 to 5 inches resulted in widespread street and basement flooding in portions of Cook, Lake, St. Louis, and Pine counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cook County law enforcement reported widespread road flooding from the evening of October 18 through early October 19. Portions of U.S. Highway 61 were flooded near Taconite Harbor. Several other county roads were also closed due to flooding, including county roads 1, 14, 42, 58, 60, 69, 70, and 71.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense area of low pressure brought heavy rains to portions of northeast Minnesota on October 18 through the morning hours of October 19. Rainfall amounts of 2 to 5 inches resulted in widespread street and basement flooding in portions of Cook, Lake, St. Louis, and Pine counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Portions of Mall Drive and Maple Grove Road were flooded and closed after Miller Creek overflowed its banks during the evening of October 18. The flood waters receded during the early morning hours of October 19. The St. Louis County sheriff also reported flooded basements and culverts in many areas across Duluth.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minnesota State Patrol reported widespread flooding in southern Lake County, between Silver Bay and Two Harbors, and near the Silver Cliff Tunnel and Split Rock Lighthouse from the afternoon of October 18 through the early morning hours of October 19. ||Water also inundated portions of Highway 61, Highway 1, and several county roads, including County Road 3 near Silver Bay and Cramer Road near Finland. The Baptism River near Tettegouche State Park was also near bankfull. Some flooding and many washed out driveways were reported in Silver Bay.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced marginally large hail in Jackson County during the morning of September 13th.

A lack of rainfall over the summer and fall of 2006 kept many counties across northwest and west central Minnesota in a D2 (severe) or D3 (extreme) drought designation (per the U.S. Drought Monitor). Roseau, Lake of the Woods, East Marshall, Pennington, North and South Beltrami, North and South Clearwater, Hubbard, and Wadena counties were classified in the D3 category. Red Lake, East Polk, Mahnomen, and East Becker counties were grouped in the D2 category.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This was the first big snow event of the winter season, and it was a Colorado Low. These are traditionally bigger snow producers for the northern plains than Alberta Clippers or hybrid low pressure systems. This system was no exception, as it dropped from 6 to 11 inches of snow over southeast North Dakota and portions of west central and northwest Minnesota. Wind speeds gusted to 30 mph frequently from Saturday evening (1st) into Sunday morning (2nd), producing blowing and drifting snow and visibilities less than a quarter mile at times in exposed areas. With the warm ground temperatures, surfaces did become slick. The highest snow total, 11 inches, occurred at Wadena, Minnesota. This event began late on November 30th and continued into the first part of this month. Since many drivers were not accustomed to winter driving yet, there were quite a few vehicle accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the heels of the first early December heavy snow producer (across southeast North Dakota and portions of northwest and west central Minnesota), a second heavy snow event followed roughly two days later. Six to 9.5 inches of snow fell along a corridor from northern Towner County (ND) to southern Grand Forks County (ND) to central Wadena County (MN). When combined with the snow totals from the prior early December event, many locations ended up with 15 to 20 inches of snow. This was good news for many snow related business in the area, which had not had this much early December snow for a number of years. As far as meteorological details, an area of surface low pressure tracked from eastern Montana to near Sioux Falls, SD. A fairly strong vorticity maximum was evident on water vapor imagery along the heavy snow path. The system also had a coupled upper jet structure to aid in providing strong lift. Quite a few rural schools cancelled classes or events early on Tuesday (4th) or early on Wednesday (5th).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roads barricaded at the intersection of County Roads 1 and 9, as well as at Highway 52 and 100th. Numerous township roads were also flooded. Near Stanton, Prairie Creek overflowed its banks and flooded a pasture. Rainfall measurements include 5.30 inches in Goodhue and 5.90 inches in Wanamingo.

A spotter eight miles north of Nicollet recorded 5.07 inches, and the cooperative observer in St. Peter registered 3.77 inches for the 24 hour period ending at 0600 CST August 19.

Water filled much of the county fairgrounds. A spotter measured 7.75 inches rain over a 24 hour period ending at 1630 CST August 19.

A 52 year old homeless man died of exposure after falling asleep in a bus shelter in Moorhead. He was found by a person walking by the bus shelter the morning of the 4th. The temperature during the late evening of the 3rd into the morning of the 4th ranged around 16 degrees, with a southeast wind at about 10 mph. The wind chill temperature at the time ranged around 5 above zero.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A north to south oriented cold front moved across Minnesota, connecting to low pressure that tracked across Iowa.  Moist southerly flow resulted in thunderstorms across portions of southern Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to five inches of rain fell over Owatonna, resulting in flooding of roads, homes and businesses.  A city storm sewer also backed up, causing basements to flood in about 40 homes. This event ended October 1, 2007 at 0300 CST (see October 2007 Storm Data). EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This was the first big snow event of the winter season, and it was a Colorado Low. These are traditionally bigger snow producers for the northern plains than Alberta Clippers or hybrid low pressure systems. This system was no exception, as it dropped from 6 to 11 inches of snow over southeast North Dakota and portions of west central and northwest Minnesota. Wind speeds never became gusty, but the with warm ground temperatures, surfaces did become slick. The highest snow total, 11 inches, occurred at Wadena, Minnesota. This event continued through December 2nd.

A cold front moved across North Dakota on the 26th, with a hot and humid airmass in place ahead of the front. At 6 pm CST, a surface low was located over southern Manitoba (Canada), just north of Pembina, ND. The cold front stretched to the southwest, back toward Devils Lake (ND) and Bismarck (ND). Ahead of the cold front, temperatures ranged in the low to mid 80s with dew points around 70F. Behind the front, temperatures did not cool significantly, but dew points dropped to the mid 40s. Thunderstorms began to form along and ahead of the cold front by early evening (from northeast ND into south central ND), eventually crossing into northwest Minnesota several hours later. Eight distinct tornadoes occurred over northeast North Dakota and northwest Minnesota during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Forty-five mph winds occurred with gusts up to 60 mph. The hail lasted from 850 to 853 pm CST. 1.58 inches of rain fell in 35 minutes.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail of 2.7 inches in diameter was measured at the intersection of Schmitt Lake Road and Highway 169. Tennis ball size hail (2.5) was estimated at the intersection of 73rd and Zane Avenues. Golf ball size hail (1.75) was estimated in the northeast corner of Brooklyn Park at the intersection of 109th Avenue and West River Road. Nickel size hail (0.88) was reported at the corner of Zane Avenue and County Road 30. Several car dealerships commented on hail damage to vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late in the evening of the 17th, an area of surface low pressure was located just northeast of Jamestown, North Dakota, with a cold front extending south from the low. A warm front arched to the northeast, from near Jamestown to Grand Forks to Bemidji (MN). From the surface through the low layers of the atmosphere, wind speeds were quite strong. In the late afternoon of the 17th, one lone thunderstorm cell developed over Polk County (MN). By early evening, another lone thunderstorm developed over Ramsey County (ND). Finally, just before midnight, a line of thunderstorms took shape over eastern North Dakota. This line of storms moved into northwest Minnesota around midnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sixty to seventy trees were snapped or uprooted at the Thief Lake Wildlife Area. Some trees blocked roads. A small shed was also blown 100 feet away. A powerline was cut to the main building.

About 1.50 to 2.50 inches of rain on the 1st, and then 6 to 9 inches of snow on the 2nd and 3rd caused more flooding across Wilkin County. Most of the problems continued along U.S. Highway 75 near Kent. The Red River at Wahpeton/Breckenridge had the highest of its multiple crests on April 2nd, topping out around 13.7 feet. A cold snap developed by Wednesday (4th), which slowed down the runoff and allowed the flood warning to be cancelled.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm moved from the southern Plains into the western Great Lakes during the weekend of December 22-23. In southeast Minnesota, this was the second high impact storm of the month, producing heavy snow and strong winds. Highest snow accumulations were near the Mississippi River and ranged between 6 and 10 inches. Specific reports from weather observers included 9.3 inches near Reno (Houston County), 9.0 inches at Winona (Winona County) and 8.3 inches at Spring Grove (Houston County). Strong northwest wind gusts of 40 to 45 mph were common, with a few locations reaching around 50 mph. This caused blowing snow, which produced whiteout conditions.||The strongest winds were closer to Interstate 35 with gusts of 52 mph at Dexter, MN (Mower County) and 51 mph at Kasson, MN (Dodge County).  Rochester, MN recorded a gust of 49 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Presidential Disaster Declaration was signed for Steele County in the wake of major flooding and damage. Large areas of Owatonna were reported under two to three feet of water. Water completely covered a few roads, and was above car doors in some areas. Maple Creek at Dartt's Park was reported three times as wide as normal, and spread out into the parking lot. Some basement walls were reported to have collapsed. Two hotels in Owatonna were evacuated. The Steele County emergency manager reported 274 homes and 8 businesses were damaged. Seven homes were considered destroyed. The cooperative observer in Owatonna measured 6.47 inches as of 0700 CST August 19. Some information provided by the Owatonna People's Press newspaper.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The lack of snow and rain contributed to extreme drought conditions continuing throught the month of January across all of northeast Minnesota. Duluth only recorded 0.20 inches of precipitation for the month, which is well below the normal amount of 1.12 inches. Season snowfall departures from normal were over 50 percent through the end of January for both Duluth and International Falls. The lack of a substantial snow pack even contributed to a 100 acre cattail fire near the Leech Lake area of Cass County on January 17th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A long and wide swath of damaging wind extended from just west of the Minnesota State Fair grounds in Falcon Heights to the northeastern corner of the city of St Paul. Approximately 4000 trees were felled.  Affected neighborhoods were St. Anthony, Como and Phalen.  The worst damage was in the Como Park area, where many trees fell on houses, vehicles, sheds and garages. One tree crashed into a house and destroyed it. One man was injured when a window blew in on him. At the State Fair, part of the grandstand roof was torn off, and roofs were also torn off some exhibition buildings.  Dozens of vendor booths were blown around, with many severely damaged.  The damage was oriented from west to east. At one point shortly after the storm, XCEL Energy reported over 250,000 outages across the Minneapolis-St. Paul region.

Several boys were playing in and along the creek, and a thirteen year old boy was swept below a nearby dam. Some of the heavy rain was funneled into a neighborhood near Hancock and Third, where six houses were flooded. A retaining wall was blown out in the Swede Hollow neighborhood.

A strong upper low, which trekked from the lower Rockies into the upper Midwest, was responsible for the heavy snow.  During the evening of the 14th, this created an unstable atmosphere aloft above south central Minnesota, and helped form a heavier band of snow from St. James to the south side of the Twin Cities. Some of the heaviest snowfall reports include: Burnsville, Sherburne and St. James (8.0 inches); Montgomery (7.5 inches); Henderson, Apple Valley, Wells (7.0 inches); Farmington and Fairmont (6.0 inches).

On the heels of the first early December heavy snow producer (across southeast North Dakota and portions of northwest and west central Minnesota), a second heavy snow event followed roughly two days later. Six to 9.5 inches of snow fell along a corridor from northern Towner County (ND) to southern Grand Forks County (ND) to central Wadena County (MN). When combined with the snow totals from the prior early December event, many locations ended up with 15 to 20 inches of snow. This was good news for many snow related business in the area, which had not had this much early December snow for a number of years. As far as meteorological details, an area of surface low pressure tracked from eastern Montana to near Sioux Falls, SD. A fairly strong vorticity maximum was evident on water vapor imagery along the heavy snow path. The system also had a coupled upper jet structure to aid in providing strong lift. Quite a few rural schools cancelled classes or events early on Tuesday (4th) or early on Wednesday (5th).

A strong low pressure system brought heavy snow to northeast Minnesota. Snow overspread the area quickly and became heavy during the mid to late morning hours of December 1. Most of northeast Minnesota, with the exception of the area from Walker to International Falls, was in the heavy snow area. Snowfall totals ranged from 5 inches in International Falls to around 20 inches in Grand Marais. Totals of 10 to 14 inches were common from the Brainerd Lakes region eastward to the north shore of Lake Superior, as well as the Arrowhead. The cities of Duluth and Hermantown received 14 to 16 inches of snow.  In addition to the heavy snow, frequent wind gusts of 30 to 40 mph near Lake Superior caused considerable blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense low pressure system brought wintry precipitation, mainly in the form of heavy snow, to much of northeast Minnesota over the pre-holiday weekened. Precipitation began as a mixture of light snow, rain, and freezing drizzle on the evening of December 21. Periods of light snow persisted during much of December 22, before turning heavy by early December 23. Storm snowfall totals ranged from 6 to 18 inches, including a broad area of 9 to 11 inches over the Arrowhead and higher amounts near Duluth. Winds gusted to 40 mph on December 23, resulting in considerable blowing and drifting snow.

Heavy lake effect snow developed over the Duluth area during the morning hours of December 4 and persisted through early evening. Snowfall rates of up to 2 inches per hour were common during the afternoon, especially in the immediate Duluth area. Snowfall totals varied considerably and ranged from nearly 18 inches in West Duluth to just 2 inches on the east end of town. The heaviest snow totals of 10 inches or more extended from West Duluth through Piedmont Heights to Proctor, Hermantown, and Adolph.

A strong low pressure area moving across the central plains brought widespread heavy snowfall of 6 to 10 inches to west central Minnesota. The snow began in the mid morning hours and ended in the late evening hours of December 1st. Travel was affected along with Saturday activities. Snowfall amounts included, 7 inches at Artichoke Lake and Tintah, 8 inches at Wheaton, and 10 inches at Ortonville.

This was the first big snow event of the winter season, and it was a Colorado Low. These are traditionally bigger snow producers for the northern plains than Alberta Clippers or hybrid low pressure systems. This system was no exception, as it dropped from 6 to 11 inches of snow over southeast North Dakota and portions of west central and northwest Minnesota. Wind speeds gusted to 30 mph frequently from Saturday evening (1st) into Sunday morning (2nd), producing blowing and drifting snow and visibilities less than a quarter mile at times in exposed areas. With the warm ground temperatures, surfaces did become slick. The highest snow total, 11 inches, occurred at Wadena, Minnesota. This event began late on November 30th and continued into the first part of this month. Since many drivers were not accustomed to winter driving yet, there were quite a few vehicle accidents.

A slow moving cold front provided the lift mechanism needed for strong thunderstorms, heavy rain and flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water flooded the intersection of Highway 67 and 68 in the city of Morgan. Water flowed out of ditches along Highway 71 near Redwood Falls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and rapid warming brought flooding to portions of central and southern Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spring snow melt and rapid warming brought ice jams and flooding to portions of central and southern Minnesota. On the 14th, large ice chunks were pushed into the pilings of an old bridge on County Road 18 in eastern Sibley County, and the bridge was deemed beyond repair.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and rapid warming brought flooding to portions of central and southern Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water from an ice jam along the Minnesota River undermined a railroad bridge over a slough near Gifford Lake, northeast of Jordan.  Five boxcars fell when the bridge collapsed.

Totals of 10 to 14 inches were common from the Brainerd Lakes region eastward to the north shore of Lake Superior, as well as the Arrowhead. The cities of Duluth and Hermantown received 14 to 16 inches of snow.  In addition to the heavy snow, frequent wind gusts of 30 to 40 mph near Lake Superior caused considerable blowing and drifting snow.

On the heels of the first early December heavy snow producer (across southeast North Dakota and portions of northwest and west central Minnesota), a second heavy snow event followed roughly two days later. Six to 9.5 inches of snow fell along a corridor from northern Towner County (ND) to southern Grand Forks County (ND) to central Wadena County (MN). When combined with the snow totals from the prior early December event, many locations ended up with 15 to 20 inches of snow. This was good news for many snow related business in the area, which had not had this much early December snow for a number of years. As far as meteorological details, an area of surface low pressure tracked from eastern Montana to near Sioux Falls, SD. A fairly strong vorticity maximum was evident on water vapor imagery along the heavy snow path. The system also had a coupled upper jet structure to aid in providing strong lift. Quite a few rural schools cancelled classes or events early on Tuesday (4th) or early on Wednesday (5th).

An intense low pressure system brought wintry precipitation, mainly in the form of heavy snow, to much of northeast Minnesota over the pre-holiday weekened. Precipitation began as a mixture of light snow, rain, and freezing drizzle on the evening of December 21. Periods of light snow persisted during much of December 22, before turning heavy by early December 23. Storm snowfall totals ranged from 6 to 18 inches, including a broad area of 9 to 11 inches over the Arrowhead and higher amounts near Duluth. Winds gusted to 40 mph on December 23, resulting in considerable blowing and drifting snow.

A Colorado Low moved across Kansas and into northern Missouri, then shifted northeast into Wisconsin. To the north of this low, an inverted trough set up into the Red River Valley. Several distinct bouts of precipitation were focused into southeast North Dakota and the northwest quarter of Minnesota during this event. Precipitation initially broke out during the night of the 23rd as light freezing rain over portions of west central Minnesota, but it quickly switched over to snow. This first bout of snow brought about 6 inches to Grant, Otter Tail, and Wadena counties by noon on the 24th. Additional bands of snow fell across the area that afternoon, but the heaviest band of snow set up over Barnes, Griggs, and Steele counties (in North Dakota), where 6 to 8 inches of snow was reported by 7 pm (on the 24th). A third round of snow fell overnight (into the morning of the 25th), covering a wide swath of area from Havana (ND) to Lancaster (MN). Many NWS cooperative observers reported 6 to 9 inches of snow that morning (25th) from this third bout of snow. Finally, the last band of snow clipped portions of west central Minnesota on Sunday (25th), with an additional 2 to 5 inches of snow. Therefore, snow totals were greatest over west central Minnesota, where Wadena reported 15.5 inches, Dalton reported 12.3 inches, and New York Mills reported 11.0 inches. Some higher totals were also received over portions of northwest Minnesota, where Lancaster reported 12.0 inches and Karlstad reported 11.0 inches.

A lack of rainfall over the summer and fall of 2006 kept many counties across northwest and west central Minnesota in a D2 (severe) or D3 (extreme) drought designation (per the U.S. Drought Monitor). Although there was snowfall in these areas over the winter of 2006-07, there was not enough to end the drought designation placed on these areas. Roseau, Lake of the Woods, East Marshall, Pennington, North and South Beltrami, North and South Clearwater, Hubbard, and Wadena counties were classified in the D3 category. Red Lake, East Polk, Mahnomen, and East Becker counties were grouped in the D2 category.

A powerful winter storm moved from Kansas to the lower Great Lakes. This storm produced a combination of significant heavy snow, sleet and ice accumulations, which virtually paralyzed southeast Minnesota. Near the Mississippi River, snow accumulations totaled 20 to 26 inches, with an isolated report of 29.5 inches near Winona (Winona County). Further west, ice accumulations of 3/4 inch to 1.5 inches affected areas mainly southwest of Rochester (Olmsted County), including Austin (Mower County) and Stewartville (Olmsted County). Adams in Mower County was especially hard hit with thick glaze ice, numerous power poles and trees downed.  A heavy glaze of ice in these locations caused power outages. Travel was dangerous or impossible througout southeast Minnesota.

As the winter storm moved across Iowa toward the lower Great Lakes, blizzard conditions developed Saturday night, February 24, and lasted into Sunday morning, February 25. Winds gusting to around 40 mph created white-out conditions, along with considerable blowing and drifting snow. In fact, snow drifts in some locations were as much as 4 to 5 feet deep. The strong winds and heavy snow delayed the restoration of electricity to numerous homes without power, which had been affected by a heavy glazing of ice Saturday morning.

Within a week of being hit by the powerful winter storm of February 23 to 25, another winter storm affected southeast Minnesota from February 28 to March 2. This storm produced snow accumulations between 6 and 12 inches. Specific reports included 11.5 inches at Claremont (Dodge County), 11.2 inches at Byron (Olmsted County) and 11.1 inches at Oronoco (Olmsted County). Strong northwest winds caused considerable blowing and drifting snow, which resulted in the closure of several roads. In addition, 1/8 inch to 1/4 inch of ice impacted areas near the Mississippi River from Wabasha (Wabasha County) to Winona (Winona County).

Near the Mississippi River, snow accumulations totaled 20 to 26 inches, with an isolated report of 29.5 inches near Winona (Winona County). Further west, ice accumulations of 3/4 inch to 1.5 inches affected areas mainly southwest of Rochester (Olmsted County), including Austin (Mower County) and Stewartville (Olmsted County). Adams in Mower County was especially hard hit with thick glaze ice, numerous power poles and trees downed.  A heavy glaze of ice in these locations caused power outages. Travel was dangerous or impossible througout southeast Minnesota.

Within a week of being hit by the powerful winter storm of February 23 to 25, another winter storm affected southeast Minnesota from February 28 to March 2. This storm produced snow accumulations between 6 and 12 inches. Specific reports included 11.5 inches at Claremont (Dodge County), 11.2 inches at Byron (Olmsted County) and 11.1 inches at Oronoco (Olmsted County). Strong northwest winds caused considerable blowing and drifting snow, which resulted in the closure of several roads. In addition, 1/8 inch to 1/4 inch of ice impacted areas near the Mississippi River from Wabasha (Wabasha County) to Winona (Winona County).

A Colorado Low moved across Kansas and into northern Missouri, then shifted northeast into Wisconsin. To the north of this low, an inverted trough set up into the Red River Valley. Several distinct bouts of precipitation were focused into southeast North Dakota and the northwest quarter of Minnesota during this event. Precipitation initially broke out during the night of the 23rd as light freezing rain over portions of west central Minnesota, but it quickly switched over to snow. This first bout of snow brought about 6 inches to Grant, Otter Tail, and Wadena counties by noon on the 24th. Additional bands of snow fell across the area that afternoon, but the heaviest band of snow set up over Barnes, Griggs, and Steele counties (in North Dakota), where 6 to 8 inches of snow was reported by 7 pm (on the 24th). A third round of snow fell overnight (into the morning of the 25th), covering a wide swath of area from Havana (ND) to Lancaster (MN). Many NWS cooperative observers reported 6 to 9 inches of snow that morning (25th) from this third bout of snow. Finally, the last band of snow clipped portions of west central Minnesota on Sunday (25th), with an additional 2 to 5 inches of snow. Therefore, snow totals were greatest over west central Minnesota, where Wadena reported 15.5 inches, Dalton reported 12.3 inches, and New York Mills reported 11.0 inches. Some higher totals were also received over portions of northwest Minnesota, where Lancaster reported 12.0 inches and Karlstad reported 11.0 inches.

A lack of rainfall over the summer and fall of 2006 kept many counties across northwest and west central Minnesota in a D2 (severe) or D3 (extreme) drought designation (per the U.S. Drought Monitor). Although there was snowfall in these areas over the winter of 2006-07, there was not enough to end the drought designation placed on these areas. Roseau, Lake of the Woods, East Marshall, Pennington, North and South Beltrami, North and South Clearwater, Hubbard, and Wadena counties were classified in the D3 category. Red Lake, East Polk, Mahnomen, and East Becker counties were grouped in the D2 category.

Cool surface high pressure settled into the western and central Dakotas, in the wake of a cold front. By the morning of February 3, temperatures ranged from -30F to -35F over northwest Minnesota.  Along with northwest wind speeds at 10 to 20 mph, wind chill temperatures dipped to -40F to -55F.

Roseau, Lake of the Woods, East Marshall, Pennington, North and South Beltrami, North and South Clearwater, Hubbard, and Wadena counties were classified in the D3 category. Red Lake, East Polk, Mahnomen, and East Becker counties were grouped in the D2 category.

A Colorado Low moved across Kansas and into northern Missouri, then shifted northeast into Wisconsin. To the north of this low, an inverted trough set up into the Red River Valley. Several distinct bouts of precipitation were focused into southeast North Dakota and the northwest quarter of Minnesota during this event. Precipitation initially broke out during the night of the 23rd as light freezing rain over portions of west central Minnesota, but it quickly switched over to snow. This first bout of snow brought about 6 inches to Grant, Otter Tail, and Wadena counties by noon on the 24th. Additional bands of snow fell across the area that afternoon, but the heaviest band of snow set up over Barnes, Griggs, and Steele counties (in North Dakota), where 6 to 8 inches of snow was reported by 7 pm (on the 24th). A third round of snow fell overnight (into the morning of the 25th), covering a wide swath of area from Havana (ND) to Lancaster (MN). Many NWS cooperative observers reported 6 to 9 inches of snow that morning (25th) from this third bout of snow. Finally, the last band of snow clipped portions of west central Minnesota on Sunday (25th), with an additional 2 to 5 inches of snow. Therefore, snow totals were greatest over west central Minnesota, where Wadena reported 15.5 inches, Dalton reported 12.3 inches, and New York Mills reported 11.0 inches. Some higher totals were also received over portions of northwest Minnesota, where Lancaster reported 12.0 inches and Karlstad reported 11.0 inches.

A Colorado Low tracked from northwest Missouri to eastern Wisconsin, while an inverted trough extended back into the Red River Valley. This brought a prolonged period of snow to eastern North Dakota and portions of northwest and west central Minnesota. The snow continued into March 1st, at which point wind speeds also increased. Blizzard conditions were reported from New Rockford to Cooperstown to Page to Wahpeton/Breckenridge. More information about this event and the blizzard event can be found on the March storm data report.

Another late February strong low pressure area moving across the central plains spread heavy snow of 7 to 9 inches into Big Stone and Traverse counties. Many schools and events were cancelled or postponed. Some vehicle accidents also occurred. The snowfall continued into the first day of March and ended between 7 and 9 am in the morning. Snowfall amounts included, 7 inches at Artichoke Lake, and 9 inches at Browns Valley and Wheaton. This heavy snow event was followed by more snowfall along with blizzard conditions which developed across the area during the afternoon and evening of March 1st. See the storm data for March 2007.

A strong upper level low pressure system moved from the Rockies on the 22nd, and across much of the Upper Midwest from the 23rd to the 25th. A large swath of heavy snow accompanied by sustained winds in the 15 to 20 mph range were noted across much of Central and South Central Minnesota. Some notable snow totals include: 17.5 inches at Hewitt (Todd County), 17.0 inches at New London (Kandiyohi County) and St. James (Watonwan County), 16.5 inches at 10 miles N of Mora (Kanabec County), 15.5 inches at Hutchinson (McLeod County), 14.0 inches at Albert Lea (Freeborn County), and 12.2 inches at Chanhassen (Carver County).

Surface low moved from Southeastern Montana to Northeast Kansas to Eastern South Dakota, during the daylight hours of the 6th. Warm frontal boundary associated with this system generated much of the snow across the region. Snowfall amounts of note include 6 inches at: Faribault, Glencoe, two miles S Gilchrist, and Hutchinson. New London, Litchfield and Donnelly each picked up 5 inches of snow.

Brutally cold temperatures combined with winds in the 10 to 20 mph range to create dangerous wind chills across northeast Minnesota in early February. Wind chills of -40 to -50 were common February 3rd through the 5th, with afternoon highs remaining below zero. The cold wave closed schools, suspended some outdoor activities, and burst pipes because of the lack of snow cover. Power companies urged their customers to conserve energy during this cold wave, as the region's electrical grid became stressed. It was the longest period of cold since 1996. Morning low temperatures at Duluth were below zero for 10 consecutive days. Lows in the -30s were common across the arrowhead during this first weekend in February into Monday, and Embarrass bottomed out at -42 on the morning of the 4th.

A mainly dry February contributed to ongoing extreme drought conditions across all of northeast Minnesota. Below normal precipitation once again occured at International Falls for the month. At Duluth, precipitation was above normal, though most of it occured at the end of February as a major winter storm brought heavy snow to an area from just south of the Iron Range, westward to the Cass and Brainerd Lakes area.

A powerful storm system moved through the upper midwest in late February, and brought heavy snow to much of northeast Minnesota with localized blizzard conditions along the north shore of Lake Superior. The snow began late on the 23rd in the Cass and Brainerd Lakes area, and ended on the evening of the 25th. Snowfall of 12 to 14 inches occured at the head of Lake Superior from Two Harbors to Cloquet, as well as across the Cass and Brainerd Lakes area, through southern Aitkin County and into western Pine County. Areas from the Iron Range to northern and eastern areas of the arrowhead received less than 2 inches. In addition to the heavy snow, blizzard conditions occured across the north shore during the early morning hours of the 25th, with winds gusting 40 to 50 mph. Many churches in Duluth cancelled Sunday morning services because of blowing and drifting snow.

Some notable snow totals include: 17.5 inches at Hewitt (Todd County), 17.0 inches at New London (Kandiyohi County) and St. James (Watonwan County), 16.5 inches at 10 miles N of Mora (Kanabec County), 15.5 inches at Hutchinson (McLeod County), 14.0 inches at Albert Lea (Freeborn County), and 12.2 inches at Chanhassen (Carver County).

Brutally cold temperatures combined with winds in the 10 to 20 mph range to create dangerous wind chills across northeast Minnesota in early February. Wind chills of -40 to -50 were common February 3rd through the 5th, with afternoon highs remaining below zero. The cold wave closed schools, suspended some outdoor activities, and burst pipes because of the lack of snow cover. Power companies urged their customers to conserve energy during this cold wave, as the region's electrical grid became stressed. It was the longest period of cold since 1996. Morning low temperatures at Duluth were below zero for 10 consecutive days. Lows in the -30s were common across the arrowhead during this first weekend in February into Monday, and Embarrass bottomed out at -42 on the morning of the 4th.

A powerful storm system moved through the upper midwest in late February, and brought heavy snow to much of northeast Minnesota with localized blizzard conditions along the north shore of Lake Superior. The snow began late on the 23rd in the Cass and Brainerd Lakes area, and ended on the evening of the 25th. Snowfall of 12 to 14 inches occured at the head of Lake Superior from Two Harbors to Cloquet, as well as across the Cass and Brainerd Lakes area, through southern Aitkin County and into western Pine County. Areas from the Iron Range to northern and eastern areas of the arrowhead received less than 2 inches. In addition to the heavy snow, blizzard conditions occured across the north shore during the early morning hours of the 25th, with winds gusting 40 to 50 mph. Many churches in Duluth cancelled Sunday morning services because of blowing and drifting snow.

A mainly dry February contributed to ongoing extreme drought conditions across all of northeast Minnesota. Below normal precipitation once again occured at International Falls for the month. At Duluth, precipitation was above normal, though most of it occured at the end of February as a major winter storm brought heavy snow to an area from just south of the Iron Range, westward to the Cass and Brainerd Lakes area.

Surface low moved from Southeastern Montana to Northeast Kansas to Eastern South Dakota, during the daylight hours of the 6th. Warm frontal boundary associated with this system generated much of the snow across the region. Snowfall amounts of note include 6 inches at: Faribault, Glencoe, two miles S Gilchrist, and Hutchinson. New London, Litchfield and Donnelly each picked up 5 inches of snow.

The second in a series of powerful winter storms that hit southeast Minnesota in late February continued through March 1 and 2. Total snow accumulations ranged between 6 and 12 inches. Specific reports included 11.5 inches at Claremont (Dodge County), 11.2 inches at Byron (Olmsted County) and 11.1 inches at Oronoco (Olmsted County). This snowfall combined with the previous storm produced snow depths of up to two and a half feet. Strong northwest winds caused considerable blowing and drifting snow, which resulted in the closure of several roads. In addition to the heavy snow, ice accumulations of 1/8 inch to 1/4 inch affected areas near the Mississippi River from Wabasha (Wabasha County) to Winona (Winona County).

Some notable snow totals include: 17.5 inches at Hewitt (Todd County), 17.0 inches at New London (Kandiyohi County) and St. James (Watonwan County), 16.5 inches at 10 miles N of Mora (Kanabec County), 15.5 inches at Hutchinson (McLeod County), 14.0 inches at Albert Lea (Freeborn County), and 12.2 inches at Chanhassen (Carver County).

Another late February strong low pressure area moving across the central plains spread heavy snow of 7 to 9 inches into Big Stone and Traverse counties. Many schools and events were cancelled or postponed. Some vehicle accidents also occurred. The snowfall continued into the first day of March and ended between 7 and 9 am in the morning. Snowfall amounts included, 7 inches at Artichoke Lake, and 9 inches at Browns Valley and Wheaton. This heavy snow event was followed by more snowfall along with blizzard conditions which developed across the area during the afternoon and evening of March 1st. See the storm data for March 2007.

A strong upper low, which trekked from the lower Rockies into the upper Midwest, was responsible for the heavy snow.  During the evening of the 14th, this created an unstable atmosphere aloft above south central Minnesota, and helped form a heavier band of snow from St. James to the south side of the Twin Cities. Some of the heaviest snowfall reports include: Burnsville, Sherburne and St. James (8.0 inches); Montgomery (7.5 inches); Henderson, Apple Valley, Wells (7.0 inches); Farmington and Fairmont (6.0 inches).

The Duluth area, Carlton County and northern Pine County were in a moderate drought (D1).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rapid warm up developed in early March after significant snowfall at the end of February and the beginning of March. The warmup resulted in a rapid snowmelt which was enough to cause minor to moderate flooding on the Rock and Redwood Rivers. Lowlands along the rivers were flooded, but no significant damage to farmland, homes, or other property was reported. No significant rain fell during the flooding period, and the flooding subsided during the middle part of March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Warm temperatures caused a rapid snowmelt which caused the Redwood River to rise above flood stage for a few days. The flooding was generally minor, and no significant damage was reported. The river crested at around 2 feet above flood stage at Marshall on March 13th, and at Russel on March 14th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall of over 20 inches combined with winds over 50 mph to create blizzard conditions across northeastern Minnesota. The storm hit hardest in the Duluth area and along the western shoreline of Lake Superior, where it will be remembered as the Blizzard of 2007. Low pressure formed over Oklahoma, strengthening while moving northeastward towards eastern Iowa and southwest Wisconsin. The area of heavy snow occurred to the northwest of the low pressure center, over northwest Wisconsin and northeast Minnesota. Light snow began falling around midnight on the 1st. Winds began picking up during the morning on Thursday, March 1st, with localized blizzard conditions beginning to be felt around the Duluth area by mid morning. Snow began falling heavily by mid afternoon as the first very intense snow bands arrived with the storm. Snowfall rates were up to 2 inches per hour during the late afternoon and evening hours of the 1st, with zero visibility occurring around Duluth and the north shore of Lake Superior. In addition, thunder was reported for about 6 hours along and east of Interstate 35. The cities of Duluth and Superior pulled snow plows off the roads by late afternoon, as travel became virtually impossible with sustained winds of 40 to 50 mph with gusts over 60 mph. Park Point was closed to all traffic late on the 1st as huge snowdrifts 10 to 15 feet high covered Minnesota Avenue. Schools and many businesses were closed March 1st and 2nd. Although areas away from Lake Superior did not experience the intense winds, snowfall totals of over 20 inches were common in most areas along and east of the Interstate 35 corridor by the time the snow tapered to flurries the night of the March 2nd. Areas near the Canadian border received very little snow.
