The initial winter storm warning for west central Minnesota was issued on the afternoon of February 27th, while the winter storm warning for the rest of northwest Minnesota was issued early in the morning on February 28th. Therefore this winter storm event covers the end of February into early March. A Colorado Low tracked from northwest Missouri to eastern Wisconsin, while an inverted trough extended back into the Red River Valley. This brought a prolonged period of snow to eastern North Dakota and portions of northwest and west central Minnesota. Many church and school activities were postponed. Sabin and Breckenridge reported 11 inches of snow, Blackduck and Thief River Falls reported 9 inches, and Argyle and Moorhead reported 8.5 inches. For the rest of the warning area, 6 to 8 inches were common. The snow continued into the evening of March 1st, at which point wind speeds also increased. Blizzard conditions were reported from Moorhead to Fergus Falls. More information about the blizzard event can be found later in this month's report.

Snow, producing heavy accumulations, was accompanied by winds gusting over 40 mph. The snow began early on March 1st after a brief respite from the previous snowfall, with winds increasing as the day went on. Blizzard conditions with widespread near zero visibility in snow and blowing snow resulted. Roads were blocked by drifting and travel was made impossible or extremely difficult. Interstate 90 was closed during the storm. Numerous businesses and schools were forced to close, and school athletic events and other activities were cancelled. Of those who did attempt to travel during the storm, several vehicles were stranded and others slid off roads. There were several power outages. The weight of the heavy snow and the winds caused several machine shed roofs to collapse in the Tyler to Marshall area, with some machinery damage resulting. The blizzard followed another significant snow 1 to 2 days before. The snow decreased on the night of March 2nd, with blizzard conditions abating by early morning on March 3rd. New snow accumulations ranged from 8 to 16 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: New snowfall of 12 to 15 inches beginning early morning on March 1st and continuing into the night of March 2nd was accompanied by sustained winds of over 30 mph at times with gusts over 40 mph. The combination of new snow, wind, and an already existing fresh snow cover resulted in a blizzard with widespread near zero visibilities. Drifting snow with the storm helped make travel impossible to extremely difficult. As a result, some who did attempt to travel became stuck or slid off roads. Schools and school activities were cancelled and numerous businesses closed. A few power outages were reported as the heavy snow and strong winds brought down power lines. The blizzard conditions abated early on the morning of March 3rd.|

A Colorado Low tracked from northwest Missouri to eastern Wisconsin, while an inverted trough extended back into the Red River Valley. This brought a prolonged period of snow to eastern North Dakota and portions of northwest and west central Minnesota. Many church and school activities were postponed. Sabin and Breckenridge reported 11 inches of snow, Blackduck and Thief River Falls reported 9 inches, and Argyle and Moorhead reported 8.5 inches. For the rest of the warning area, 6 to 8 inches were common. The snow continued into the evening of March 1st, at which point wind speeds also increased. Blizzard conditions were reported from Moorhead to Fergus Falls. More information about the blizzard event can be found later in this month's report.

Low pressure moving slowly from Missouri to Illinois brought heavy snow across portions of southeast Minnesota. Snowfall totals ranged from 4 to 9 inches, with heaviest amounts over higher terrain. Some of the highest totals included 9.0 inches at Grand Meadow (Mower County), 7.4 inches at Rochester International Airport (Olmsted County) and 6.0 inches at Dodge Center (Dodge County). Strong northerly winds accompanied the storm, which created blowing and drifting snow.

Heavy snowfall of over 20 inches combined with winds over 50 mph to create blizzard conditions across northeastern Minnesota. The storm hit hardest in the Duluth area and along the western shoreline of Lake Superior, where it will be remembered as the Blizzard of 2007. Low pressure formed over Oklahoma, strengthening while moving northeastward towards eastern Iowa and southwest Wisconsin. The area of heavy snow occurred to the northwest of the low pressure center, over northwest Wisconsin and northeast Minnesota. Light snow began falling around midnight on the 1st. Winds began picking up during the morning on Thursday, March 1st, with localized blizzard conditions beginning to be felt around the Duluth area by mid morning. Snow began falling heavily by mid afternoon as the first very intense snow bands arrived with the storm. Snowfall rates were up to 2 inches per hour during the late afternoon and evening hours of the 1st, with zero visibility occurring around Duluth and the north shore of Lake Superior. In addition, thunder was reported for about 6 hours along and east of Interstate 35. The cities of Duluth and Superior pulled snow plows off the roads by late afternoon, as travel became virtually impossible with sustained winds of 40 to 50 mph with gusts over 60 mph. Park Point was closed to all traffic late on the 1st as huge snowdrifts 10 to 15 feet high covered Minnesota Avenue. Schools and many businesses were closed March 1st and 2nd. Although areas away from Lake Superior did not experience the intense winds, snowfall totals of over 20 inches were common in most areas along and east of the Interstate 35 corridor by the time the snow tapered to flurries the night of the March 2nd. Areas near the Canadian border received very little snow.

A strong area of low pressure moved slowly northeast across the central and northern plains bringing widespread snowfall along with intense winds. The combination of the falling snow and the existing snow cover resulted in blizzard conditions with visibilities to near zero at times. This blizzard event was part of the same upper level low pressure trough that brought the heavy snowfall to the area on February 28th. See February Storm Data. Additional snowfall occurred on March 1st and 2nd across the area as a large area of snowfall wrapped in from the east. Widespread blizzard conditions developed by noon on March 2nd and continued into the early morning hours of the 3rd. Snowfall amounts of 7 to 14 inches fell with 25 to 30 inches on the ground. Northwest winds of 25 to 45 mph brought visibilities to near zero at times along with large drifts. Schools and buisnesses were closed for a couple days. Travel was not advised and several roads were closed.

A cold front moved slowly eastward over southeast Minnesota during the late evening hours of September 30th. Thunderstorms developed along and ahead of the front, producing rainfall amounts of 3 to 5 inches in about two hours. Law enforcement officials reported localized flash flooding across the northern part of Dodge County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall caused flooding over County Roads 24, 5, and 1. Cars stalled in flood waters and local fire department personnel had to evacuate people from nearby homes and farm, in addition to the rescue of stranded motorists.

Numerous surface frontal boundaries across west central Minnesota and a developing low level jet helped ignite scattered thunderstorms.

The second in a series of powerful winter storms that hit southeast Minnesota in late February continued through March 1 and 2. Total snow accumulations ranged between 6 and 12 inches. Specific reports included 11.5 inches at Claremont (Dodge County), 11.2 inches at Byron (Olmsted County) and 11.1 inches at Oronoco (Olmsted County). This snowfall combined with the previous storm produced snow depths of up to two and a half feet. Strong northwest winds caused considerable blowing and drifting snow, which resulted in the closure of several roads. In addition to the heavy snow, ice accumulations of 1/8 inch to 1/4 inch affected areas near the Mississippi River from Wabasha (Wabasha County) to Winona (Winona County).

A deep area of low pressure moved from the Oklahoma panhandle on the evening of the 28th, to far northeastern Iowa on the evening of March 1st. The storm moved across southern Wisconsin during the morning hours of the 2nd. Some heavy snow totals of note include: 20 inches at Andover (Anoka County), 20 inches in Cambridge (Isanti County), 19.5 inches one mile southwest of Hutchinson (McLeod County), 19.0 inches in East Bethel (Anoka County), 18.0 inches in Chaska (Carver County), 18.0 inches in Minnetonka (Hennepin County), 17.0 inches in St. James (Watonwan County), and 15.0 inches in Mankato (Blue Earth County). Northerly winds in the 15 to 20 mph sustained range, along with gusts of 25 to 35 mph, caused some blowing and drifting snow. Many roads were closed or impassable, and hundreds of schools closed.

A tight surface pressure gradient set up across eastern North Dakota and portions of the northwest quarter of Minnesota, between an area of surface low pressure over west central South Dakota and a high north of the Great Lakes. Evening upper air observations on the 5th and morning upper air observations on the 6th measured 60 to 70 mph wind speeds just off the surface. Some of these 60 to 70 mph wind speeds were pushed down to the surface across southeast North Dakota and portions of west central Minnesota, causing scattered tree and property damage. Rain showers helped to mix the stronger winds down to the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest wind gusts were measured by a MNDOT RWIS sensor about 5 miles east of Moorhead between 639 and 652 am CST. An AWOS at the Moorhead Airport (on the southeast edge of town) measured a 58 mph wind gust at 635 am CST. 5 miles northeast of Barnesville, a 70 foot tall elm tree was snapped about 15 feet off the ground, breaking nearby powerlines as it fell around 445 am CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent low pressure system moved out of the Southern Rockies early on the 1st, and then trekked into the Central Great Plains by midday, and then into Eastern Iowa by the late evening hours. Some notable snow amounts include: twelve inches at Hewitt, 10 inches at Carlos, 8 inches in Little Falls, 7.7 inches in Forest Lake, 7.2 inches at Rush City, 7.0 inches in Red Wing, 6.5 inches at Glenwood, 6.4 inches in Chanhassen, 6.0 inches in Eagan, 6.0 inches in Plymouth, 6.0 inches in Zumbrota, 6.0 inches in St Paul, 5.5 inches in Long Prairie, and 5.3 inches in Waconia. Sustained winds of between 20 and 35 mph were also observed during the storm, causing considerable blowing and drifting. A few locations, especially south of a Wood Lake to Hastings line, received sleet between one eighth and three quarters of an inch.

Rainfall was persistent across southeast North Dakota and portions of west central Minnesota throughout the month of May. NWS Cooperative Observers reported May totals of 7.52 inches at Wahpeton/Breckenridge and 7.44 inches at Rothsay. These values were over 200 percent of normal. From June 1st into the 2nd, a nearly stacked low pressure system tracked across South Dakota into southwest Minnesota. Showers and thunderstorms pivoted around the low as it tracked to the east, bringing substantial rainfall amounts to extreme southeast North Dakota and portions of west central Minnesota. Rainfall totals from these two days amounted to 4.73 inches at Wahpeton/Breckenridge and 1.97 inches at Rothsay.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Wahpeton/Breckenridge, an estimate 20 to 40 homes had basement flooding or sewer backup. The Wilkin County Emergency Manager estimated at least one road in every township had washed out. On the Red River at Wahpeton/Breckenridge, which was showing a level of about 9.3 feet on the morning of the 1st, the river stage shot up to around 15.6 feet by late in the afternoon of the 3rd (a jump of over 6 feet in about 2 days). Again, the 15.6 river peak would qualify as a top ten historical crest. On the Rabbit River at Campbell, which was showing a level of about 5 feet on the evening of the 1st, the river jumped to around 17.9 feet by the morning of the 3rd (a rise of about 13 feet in a day and a half).

Snow melt and rapid warming brought flooding to portions of central and southern Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spring snow melt with rapid warming brought ice jams and flooding to portions of West Central Minnesota. On the 14th, Lac Qui Parle county officials reported flooded township roads west of the intersection of roads 212 and 75 (along the West Fork of the Lac Qui Parle River). The ice jam responsible for the flooding was west of the city of Dawson.  The Dawson Sentinel reported plenty of damage to the Helgeson Bridge southeast of Dawson. Three quarters of the bridge was toppled.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure system progressed from Nebraska to Iowa from the 2nd to early on the 3rd, and then trekked from Eastern Iowa to Northeast Wisconsin by the evening hours on the 3rd. Snowfall amounts of note include: 8.5 inches at Donnelly (Stevens county); 7.0 inches at Little Falls (Morrison county); 6.2 inches in Onamia (Mille Lacs County), 6.0 inches at Long Prairie (Todd County); 6.0 inches at Glenwood (Pope County); 6.0 inches at Cyrus (Pope County); 5.9 inches 7 miles S Hillman (Morrison county). Sustained winds in the 18 to 25 mph range, with some gusts to 35 mph, created some blowing and drifting snow.

A tight surface pressure gradient set up across eastern North Dakota and portions of the northwest quarter of Minnesota, between an area of surface low pressure over west central South Dakota and a high north of the Great Lakes. Evening upper air observations on the 5th and morning upper air observations on the 6th measured 60 to 70 mph wind speeds just off the surface. Some of these 60 to 70 mph wind speeds were pushed down to the surface across southeast North Dakota and portions of west central Minnesota, causing scattered tree and property damage. Rain showers helped to mix the stronger winds down to the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind gust was measured by a MNDOT RWIS sensor.

Northerly winds in the 15 to 20 mph sustained range, along with gusts of 25 to 35 mph, caused some blowing and drifting snow. Many roads were closed or impassable, and hundreds of schools closed.
Snow melt and rapid warming brought flooding to portions of central and southern Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water flooded Ramsey Park, covering a road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow melt and rapid warming brought flooding to portions of central and southern Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spring snow melt and rapid warming brought ice jams and flooding to portions of Southern Minnesota.  Lowland areas near the Cottonwood River in New Ulm caused flooding in this area from the 13th to the 21st, including Cottonwood Street. Just southwest of Springfield, the Cottonwood River rose nearly ten feet during the afternoon and evening on the 12th, but no damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strengthening low pressure system moved from Western South Dakota on the morning of the 10th, to southern Iowa early on the 11th, and trekked into Southern Wisconsin by early on the 12th. Sustained winds in the 15 to 20 mph range were common, with gust to about 25 mph, which caused some blowing and drifting snow. Some snowfall amounts of note include: 8.0 inches at Fairmont (Martin county); 7.0 inches at Glenville (Freeborn county); 7.0 inches at Kiester (Faribault county); and 6.0 inches at Welcome (Martin county).

A surface low pressure system progressed from Nebraska to Iowa from the 2nd to early on the 3rd, and then trekked from Eastern Iowa to Northeast Wisconsin by the evening hours on the 3rd. Snowfall amounts of note include: 8.5 inches at Donnelly (Stevens county); 7.0 inches at Little Falls (Morrison county); 6.2 inches in Onamia (Mille Lacs County), 6.0 inches at Long Prairie (Todd County); 6.0 inches at Glenwood (Pope County); 6.0 inches at Cyrus (Pope County); 5.9 inches 7 miles S Hillman (Morrison county). Sustained winds in the 18 to 25 mph range, with some gusts to 35 mph, created some blowing and drifting snow.

A deep area of low pressure moved from the Oklahoma panhandle on the evening of the 28th, to far northeastern Iowa on the evening of March 1st. The storm moved across southern Wisconsin during the morning hours of the 2nd. Some heavy snow totals of note include: 20 inches at Andover (Anoka County), 20 inches in Cambridge (Isanti County), 19.5 inches one mile southwest of Hutchinson (McLeod County), 19.0 inches in East Bethel (Anoka County), 18.0 inches in Chaska (Carver County), 18.0 inches in Minnetonka (Hennepin County), 17.0 inches in St. James (Watonwan County), and 15.0 inches in Mankato (Blue Earth County). Northerly winds in the 15 to 20 mph sustained range, along with gusts of 25 to 35 mph, caused some blowing and drifting snow. Many roads were closed or impassable, and hundreds of schools closed.

A potent low pressure system moved out of the Southern Rockies early on the 1st, and then trekked into the Central Great Plains by midday, and then into Eastern Iowa by the late evening hours. Some notable snow amounts include: twelve inches at Hewitt, 10 inches at Carlos, 8 inches in Little Falls, 7.7 inches in Forest Lake, 7.2 inches at Rush City, 7.0 inches in Red Wing, 6.5 inches at Glenwood, 6.4 inches in Chanhassen, 6.0 inches in Eagan, 6.0 inches in Plymouth, 6.0 inches in Zumbrota, 6.0 inches in St Paul, 5.5 inches in Long Prairie, and 5.3 inches in Waconia. Sustained winds of between 20 and 35 mph were also observed during the storm, causing considerable blowing and drifting. A few locations, especially south of a Wood Lake to Hastings line, received sleet between one eighth and three quarters of an inch.

As a cold front moved into southeast Minnesota during the afternoon of June 7, it triggered a cluster of severe thunderstorms. Most storm damage was in areas south of a line from Winona to Preston. Law enforcement officials, amateur radio operators and storm spotters reported wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph, which knocked down trees. A shed and garage were also damaged near Rushford (Fillmore County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines and trees were blown down.

Some notable snow amounts include: twelve inches at Hewitt, 10 inches at Carlos, 8 inches in Little Falls, 7.7 inches in Forest Lake, 7.2 inches at Rush City, 7.0 inches in Red Wing, 6.5 inches at Glenwood, 6.4 inches in Chanhassen, 6.0 inches in Eagan, 6.0 inches in Plymouth, 6.0 inches in Zumbrota, 6.0 inches in St Paul, 5.5 inches in Long Prairie, and 5.3 inches in Waconia. Sustained winds of between 20 and 35 mph were also observed during the storm, causing considerable blowing and drifting. A few locations, especially south of a Wood Lake to Hastings line, received sleet between one eighth and three quarters of an inch.

Thunderstorms produced large hail over far southwestern Minnesota in the Jasper area during the early evening of May 8th. The hail fell near the Pipestone and Rock County line. A few hailstones up to golf ball size were reported.

By early evening, a weak surface low pressure area was located between Bismarck and Jamestown (ND), with a warm front arching northeast to Grafton (ND) and then toward the Upper and Lower Red Lakes in Minnesota. Thunderstorms produced some hail and strong winds north of the surface warm front.

By mid afternoon on the 21st, an area of surface low pressure was located in southwest North Dakota, with a warm front arching northeast toward Devils Lake (ND) and then southeast toward Detroit Lakes (MN). This front basically held in place through the early evening, and was marked by a strong thermal gradient. At 6 pm CST, Jamestown reported a temperature of 82 degrees, Devils Lake reported 75 degrees, and Minot reported 59 degrees.

A cold front triggered scattered severe thunderstorms during the late afternoon and early evening of June 21. Thunderstorm wind gusts of up to 70 mph heavily damaged a farm building near Stewartville (Olmsted County), tossing debris more than 300 yards. In Taopi (Mower County), a downburst caused extensive damage to a few homes, a garage, and flipped a parked boat. Hail up to size of quarters was also reported across the area by storm spotters, law enforcement officials and the public.

A strong surface pressure gradient was located ahead of the low, helping to create gusty south southwest winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Around 3 pm CST, a fairly strong cold front was located along a line from Devils Lake, ND, to Hallock, MN. Throughout the evening, this cold front slowly sagged southward. By 6 pm CST, the front had reached a line from New Rockford, ND, to Roseau, MN. At this time, Jamestown, ND, reported a temperature of 86 degrees, while Devils Lake had dropped to 66 degrees. By midnight, the front was located along a line from Bemidji (MN) to Fargo (ND) to 20 miles south of Jamestown (ND). Behind the front, the temperature dropped to 39 degrees in Devils Lake a couple hours after midnight. Large hail, tornadoes, and flash flooding occurred during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Streets were flooded in the city of Moorhead. At the Moorhead Airport, taxiways and runways were also flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms near Lake Vermillion produced flash flooding during the morning of May 21st. National Weather Service Doppler radar estimated 4 to 6 inches of rain fell in 3 hours, and a report of 1.5 inches in 1 hour was received. Water was reported covering Kabetogama Road as well as area parking lots in Gheen. Warm advection showers and thunderstorms passed through northeast Minnesota during the afternoon and early evening of the same day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large oaks and powerlines were blown down.

A surface low pressure system progressed from Nebraska to Iowa from the 2nd to early on the 3rd, and then trekked from Eastern Iowa to Northeast Wisconsin by the evening hours on the 3rd. Snowfall amounts of note include: 8.5 inches at Donnelly (Stevens county); 7.0 inches at Little Falls (Morrison county); 6.2 inches in Onamia (Mille Lacs County), 6.0 inches at Long Prairie (Todd County); 6.0 inches at Glenwood (Pope County); 6.0 inches at Cyrus (Pope County); 5.9 inches 7 miles S Hillman (Morrison county). Sustained winds in the 18 to 25 mph range, with some gusts to 35 mph, created some blowing and drifting snow.

A deep area of low pressure moved from the Oklahoma panhandle on the evening of the 28th, to far northeastern Iowa on the evening of March 1st. The storm moved across southern Wisconsin during the morning hours of the 2nd. Some heavy snow totals of note include: 20 inches at Andover (Anoka County), 20 inches in Cambridge (Isanti County), 19.5 inches one mile southwest of Hutchinson (McLeod County), 19.0 inches in East Bethel (Anoka County), 18.0 inches in Chaska (Carver County), 18.0 inches in Minnetonka (Hennepin County), 17.0 inches in St. James (Watonwan County), and 15.0 inches in Mankato (Blue Earth County). Northerly winds in the 15 to 20 mph sustained range, along with gusts of 25 to 35 mph, caused some blowing and drifting snow. Many roads were closed or impassable, and hundreds of schools closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of showers and thunderstorms moved across western and central North Dakota, then stalled as they reached southeast North Dakota and portions of northwest Minnesota. Although the rainfall did thin out several times, it fell nearly continuously over this same area from the 13th through the 14th. An estimated 5 to 6 inches of rain fell from Fosston to Gully to Oklee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Basement flooding was reported due to the steady rain.

The month of May saw some slight improvement in the drought across northeast Minnesota.  Extreme (D3) drought conditions were scaled back to far northern areas along the international border, from Koochiching to Lake County.  Meanwhile, the severe (D2) drought area also was scaled back to include Koochiching, northern St. Louis, and much of Lake and Cook counties.  Most areas across the region received 3 to 4 inches of rain in May, which is one half to one inch above normal for the month.  Rivers and streams were running near normal to slightly below normal across the region.

A cold front, upper level shortwave trough, and lake breeze set off thunderstorms on the afternoon of May 14th.  Widespread large hail fell from northwest of Duluth in the Twig, Caribou Lake and Pike Lake areas and in Duluth, east of the Piedmont Heights neighborhood. The hail dented cars and damaged roofs of houses. Total damage was roughly estimated at $700,000.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball size hail fell at the Duluth NWS office.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The initial winter storm warning for west central Minnesota was issued on the afternoon of February 27th, while the winter storm warning for the rest of northwest Minnesota was issued early in the morning on February 28th. Therefore this winter storm event covers the end of February into early March. A Colorado Low tracked from northwest Missouri to eastern Wisconsin, while an inverted trough extended back into the Red River Valley. This brought a prolonged period of snow to eastern North Dakota and portions of northwest and west central Minnesota. Many church and school activities were postponed. Sabin and Breckenridge reported 11 inches of snow, Blackduck and Thief River Falls reported 9 inches, and Argyle and Moorhead reported 8.5 inches. For the rest of the warning area, 6 to 8 inches were common. The snow continued into the evening of March 1st, at which point wind speeds also increased. Blizzard conditions were reported from Moorhead to Fergus Falls. More information about the blizzard event can be found later in this month's report.

Some heavy snow totals of note include: 20 inches at Andover (Anoka County), 20 inches in Cambridge (Isanti County), 19.5 inches one mile southwest of Hutchinson (McLeod County), 19.0 inches in East Bethel (Anoka County), 18.0 inches in Chaska (Carver County), 18.0 inches in Minnetonka (Hennepin County), 17.0 inches in St. James (Watonwan County), and 15.0 inches in Mankato (Blue Earth County). Northerly winds in the 15 to 20 mph sustained range, along with gusts of 25 to 35 mph, caused some blowing and drifting snow. Many roads were closed or impassable, and hundreds of schools closed.

A major forest fire broke out near Ham Lake on May 5th due to an unattended campfire, and was called the Ham Lake Fire.  The fire spread north across the upper portions of the Gunflint Trail, and into Canada, destroying 61 homes on the Minnesota side, and slightly more smaller structures.  Property damage was estimated at 4 million dollars.  The fire was 100% contained on the U.S. side on May 19th, and had consumed more than 36,000 acres in Minnesota (75,000 acres total, including Canada).  This would make the Ham Lake Fire even larger than last July's Cavity Lake Fire, which up until now was the largest fire in the area in 100 years.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The initial winter storm warning for west central Minnesota was issued on the afternoon of February 27th, while the winter storm warning for the rest of northwest Minnesota was issued early in the morning on February 28th. Therefore this winter storm event covers the end of February into early March. A Colorado Low tracked from northwest Missouri to eastern Wisconsin, while an inverted trough extended back into the Red River Valley. This brought a prolonged period of snow to eastern North Dakota and portions of northwest and west central Minnesota. Many church and school activities were postponed. Sabin and Breckenridge reported 11 inches of snow, Blackduck and Thief River Falls reported 9 inches, and Argyle and Moorhead reported 8.5 inches. For the rest of the warning area, 6 to 8 inches were common. The snow continued into the evening of March 1st, at which point wind speeds also increased. Blizzard conditions were reported from Moorhead to Fergus Falls. More information about the blizzard event can be found later in this month's report.

The month of May saw some slight improvement in the drought across northeast Minnesota.  Extreme (D3) drought conditions were scaled back to far northern areas along the international border, from Koochiching to Lake County.  Meanwhile, the severe (D2) drought area also was scaled back to include Koochiching, northern St. Louis, and much of Lake and Cook counties.  Most areas across the region received 3 to 4 inches of rain in May, which is one half to one inch above normal for the month.  Rivers and streams were running near normal to slightly below normal across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms near Lake Vermillion produced flash flooding during the morning of May 21st. National Weather Service Doppler radar estimated 4 to 6 inches of rain fell in 3 hours, and a report of 1.5 inches in 1 hour was received. Water was reported covering Kabetogama Road as well as area parking lots in Gheen. Warm advection showers and thunderstorms passed through northeast Minnesota during the afternoon and early evening of the same day.
Rapid snow melt caused the Zumbro River at Zumbro Falls (Wabasha County) to reach a crest of 20.11 feet, which was a little over two feet above flood stage. A few roads were closed due to high water.

Low amounts of precipitation over the course of March 2007 kept many counties across northwest and west central Minnesota in a D2 (severe) or D3 (extreme) drought designation (per the U.S. Drought Monitor). Although there were three distinct precipitation events in March (early, mid, and late month), this was not enough moisture to end the drought designation placed on most counties. Roseau, Lake of the Woods, North and South Beltrami, North Clearwater, and Hubbard counties remained in the D3 designation all month. East Marshall, Pennington, South Clearwater, and Wadena counties began the month in a D3 designation, and then dropped down to a D2 designation on March 6th, where they remained through the rest of the month. Red Lake, East Polk, Mahnomen, and East Becker counties remained in a D2 designation all month. Kittson and West Marshall counties began the month in a D2 designation, but were dropped from the D2 designation on March 6.

Drought conditions that began last summer continued over much of northeastern Minnesota. Extreme(D3) drought conditions persisted from points just north of the Iron Range, to the Canadian border, including much of the interior Arrowhead of Minnesota. Severe(D2) drought existed in an area from Lake Winnibigoshish, to just north of Duluth, to much of the north shore of Lake Superior.

Windy conditions occurred in response to a deep low pressure center over Central South Dakota, combined with a large high pressure area over the Eastern Great Lakes. Some measured winds gusts of note include: 71 mph in Hanley Falls (Yellow Medicine County), 63 mph in Madison (Lac Qui Parle County), 59 mph in Canby (Yellow Medicine County), 60 mph in Appleton (Swift County), 68 mph four miles southwest of Belle Plaine (Scott County), 58 mph seven miles south of Starbuck (Pope County), 63 mph in Mankato (Blue Earth County), and 65 mph two miles WSW of New Prague (Le Sueur County). Scattered reports of trees, large branches, and power lines down throughout the region were reported. Additionally, strong winds ripped the roof off a repair shop in St. Clair.  Shoreland Golf Course in St. Peter had so many downed trees they were forced to close for the day; the course superintendant said it was some of the worst damage he had ever seen. According to the Pope County Sheriff's office, an evergreen tree was knocked down over wires in Starbuck, a power pole was down on trees in Lowry, a shed was blown down in Cyrus, a power pole was toppled in Glenwood, and wires were down in Starbuck. According to the Mankato Free Press newspaper, several power lines blew over, and fallen trees blocked roads in the city of Mankato. Siding was ripped off a home in North Mankato. The State Patrol confirmed a recreational vehicle was overturned on Highway 22 about 5 miles north of Mankato. In New Prague, high winds toppled several trees and tree limbs, along with a power line, which affected customers northeast of the city.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured at the Montevideo airport. A peak gust of 48 knots was also measured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lack of substantial rainfall during the months of June and July resulted in a D2 (Severe) Drought across portions of west central Minnesota (on the U.S. Drought Monitor). At New York Mills (in eastern Otter Tail County), only 2.84 inches of rain was recorded for June through July. At another NWS Cooperative Observer site 3 miles south of Wadena (in southern Wadena County), only 2.87 inches of rain fell through the same time period. The U.S. Secretary of Agriculture declared Hubbard and Wadena Counties as Federal Agricultural Disaster areas. Grant County (as well as Otter Tail, Becker, Clearwater, and Beltrami Counties) was eligible for assistance as well, as it was adjacent to a primary agricultural disaster area.

An area of thunderstorms developed across west central Minnesota late in the evening on the 10th, near an outflow boundary moving out of northeast South Dakota. These thunderstorms tracked across northern Grant County, through Alexandria, and into central Minnesota, where they produced 70 mph wind gusts. These storms were also sustained by the low level jet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many trees were blown down around Pomme de Terre Lake.

Shoreland Golf Course in St. Peter had so many downed trees they were forced to close for the day; the course superintendant said it was some of the worst damage he had ever seen. According to the Pope County Sheriff's office, an evergreen tree was knocked down over wires in Starbuck, a power pole was down on trees in Lowry, a shed was blown down in Cyrus, a power pole was toppled in Glenwood, and wires were down in Starbuck. According to the Mankato Free Press newspaper, several power lines blew over, and fallen trees blocked roads in the city of Mankato. Siding was ripped off a home in North Mankato. The State Patrol confirmed a recreational vehicle was overturned on Highway 22 about 5 miles north of Mankato. In New Prague, high winds toppled several trees and tree limbs, along with a power line, which affected customers northeast of the city.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Frontal boundary induced severe weather episode.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service damage survey confirmed that the swath of damage in Plymouth was produced by a thunderstorm downburst.  Several thousands of trees, many over one foot in diameter, were toppled across Plymouth. Five people received minor injuries and were treated at a nearby hospital. Two of the injuries occurred when two construction trailers were rolled near Highway 55 and Interstate 494.  Many utility poles were also snapped in half. The roof was partially blown off a business one half mile southwest of the 494/55 intersection.  Minor roof and siding damage to homes and businesses were also reported, and several chimneys were toppled. Xcel Energy reported that 14,000 people were left without power for several hours after the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The long-term drought across much of northeast Minnesota continued to improve during June, especially from Koochiching to Cook counties along the international border, where extreme (D3) drought conditions were upgraded to moderate (D1) by month's end. Runoff, river and stream flow continued to run near to slightly below normal across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful low pressure area moved through central Wisconsin in early April, spreading heavy snow to much of northeastern Minnesota. The snow began during the early morning hours of the 3rd, and ended by late that evening. The heaviest snow fell across the north shore of Lake Superior through northern Pine County. A foot or more was recorded in these areas, with Silver Bay reporting 13.3 inches. 12.1 inches fell at the Duluth NWS, which was not only a record for the date, but also a record for any day in the month of April for the Port City. Lesser amounts of 6 to 10 inches fell in the interior Arrowhead region, including the Iron Range, as well as the Cass and Brainerd Lakes areas. In addition to the heavy snow, strong winds of over 20 mph created blowing and drifting conditions.

In New Prague, high winds toppled several trees and tree limbs, along with a power line, which affected customers northeast of the city.

Sustained winds of 41 mph were reported at 09:04 AM with additional reports of sustained winds greater then 40 mph which continued until 0937 AM. Additional wind gusts of 62 and 65 mph were reported commencing at 0902 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful low pressure area moved through central Wisconsin in early April, spreading heavy snow to much of northeastern Minnesota. The snow began during the early morning hours of the 3rd, and ended by late that evening. The heaviest snow fell across the north shore of Lake Superior through northern Pine County. A foot or more was recorded in these areas, with Silver Bay reporting 13.3 inches. 12.1 inches fell at the Duluth NWS, which was not only a record for the date, but also a record for any day in the month of April for the Port City. Lesser amounts of 6 to 10 inches fell in the interior Arrowhead region, including the Iron Range, as well as the Cass and Brainerd Lakes areas. In addition to the heavy snow, strong winds of over 20 mph created blowing and drifting conditions.

Drought conditions that began last summer continued over much of northeastern Minnesota. Extreme(D3) drought conditions persisted from points just north of the Iron Range, to the Canadian border, including much of the interior Arrowhead of Minnesota. Severe(D2) drought existed in an area from Lake Winnibigoshish, to just north of Duluth, to much of the north shore of Lake Superior.

Late in the evening of the 17th, an area of surface low pressure was located just northeast of Jamestown, North Dakota, with a cold front extending south from the low. A warm front arched to the northeast, from near Jamestown to Grand Forks to Bemidji (MN). From the surface through the low layers of the atmosphere, wind speeds were quite strong. In the late afternoon of the 17th, one lone thunderstorm cell developed over Polk County (MN). By early evening, another lone thunderstorm developed over Ramsey County (ND). Finally, just before midnight, a line of thunderstorms took shape over eastern North Dakota. This line of storms moved into northwest Minnesota around midnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At Lake Itasca State Park, many trees were blown down along the east and north entry roads and along the wilderness trail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lack of substantial rainfall during the months of June and July resulted in a D2 (Severe) Drought across portions of west central Minnesota (on the U.S. Drought Monitor). At New York Mills (in eastern Otter Tail County), only 2.84 inches of rain was recorded for June through July. At another NWS Cooperative Observer site 3 miles south of Wadena (in southern Wadena County), only 2.87 inches of rain fell through the same time period. The U.S. Secretary of Agriculture declared Hubbard and Wadena Counties as Federal Agricultural Disaster areas. Grant County (as well as Otter Tail, Becker, Clearwater, and Beltrami Counties) was eligible for assistance as well, as it was adjacent to a primary agricultural disaster area.

A high wind event occurred across portions of eastern North Dakota and northwest Minnesota in the early morning hours of the 10th. Several things came together to cause this event. First, a strengthening upper level jet moved into the area along with sinking motion in the low to mid layers of the atmosphere. A morning upper air sounding from Aberdeen, South Dakota, showed nearly unidirectional winds from the surface to the jet level. Also at the surface, an area of low pressure located near Cooperstown (ND) in the early morning hours strengthened as it moved toward Baudette (MN) around sunrise. Some decaying weak showers could also have helped mix some of the stronger winds down to the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind gust was measured by an AWOS sensor at the Moorhead airport. Downed trees and tree branches onto powerlines caused roughly 6000 customers to lose power in the Fargo/Moorhead area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm formed in northeast North Dakota and moved into western Polk County (MN), where it produced several funnel cloud sightings.
A thunderstorm formed in northeast North Dakota and moved into western Polk County (MN), where it produced several funnel cloud sightings.

By noon on the 7th, a surface low was located near Lake Park, Minnesota, with a trailing cold front extending back into extreme southeast North Dakota. An occluded front also extended to the northeast, toward the Bemidji, MN, area. Surface dew points pooled right around 60 F in the vicinity of the surface low with temperatures in the middle 70s. The cold front was strong for early June, with a noon temperature in Devils Lake, ND, of 48 degrees. This set up a strong surface thermal and moisture boundary along a Wahpeton-Breckenridge to Bemidji line. Storms initially fired across southeast ND and then spread into portions of west central and northwest MN. There was also strong upper level support for these storms, as one severe report north of Embden (in Cass County, ND) occurred well behind the surface boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado touched down just south of county road 10 to the west of Elizabeth and tracked north-northeast about 6 miles to about 2 miles south of Erhard. Several trees were torn down and a few power poles were snapped. Spotters noted that the tornado was up and down along this path. The tornado crossed Highway 59 about 2.7 miles north of Elizabeth. Peak winds were estimated at 90 to 95 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of showers and thunderstorms moved across western and central North Dakota, then stalled as they reached southeast North Dakota and portions of northwest Minnesota. Although the rainfall did thin out several times, it fell nearly continuously over this same area from the 13th through the 14th. An estimated 5 to 6 inches of rain fell from Fosston to Gully to Oklee.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The NWS Cooperative Observer at Fosston reported 5.13 inches of rain between the 13th and 14th. Some basement flooding was reported in Erskine and Fosston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late in the evening on the 6th, an area of surface low pressure was located in north central South Dakota, with a warm front arching into extreme northeast South Dakota and then into southwest Minnesota. The low level jetstream increased in the late evening, causing showers and thunderstorms to form north of the warm front.

By noon on the 7th, a surface low was located near Lake Park, Minnesota, with a trailing cold front extending back into extreme southeast North Dakota. An occluded front also extended to the northeast, toward the Bemidji, MN, area. Surface dew points pooled right around 60 F in the vicinity of the surface low with temperatures in the middle 70s. The cold front was strong for early June, with a noon temperature in Devils Lake, ND, of 48 degrees. Storms initially fired across southeast ND and then spread into portions of west central and northwest MN. There was also strong upper level support for these storms, as one severe report north of Embden (in Cass County, ND) occurred well behind the surface boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down near the southwest end of Lake Lida, crossed Lake Lida and Crystal Lake (moving north-northeast), and lifted around 4 miles west of Vergas. Several houses were structurally damaged on Lake Lida and Crystal Lake. Boats, docks, travel trailers, and mobile homes were lofted and completely destroyed. Numerous power poles and trees were snapped off, cutting power to nearly 400 customers. Peak winds were estimated at 130 to 135 mph.

Windy conditions occurred in response to a deep low pressure center over Central South Dakota, combined with a large high pressure area over the Eastern Great Lakes. Some measured winds gusts of note include: 71 mph in Hanley Falls (Yellow Medicine County), 63 mph in Madison (Lac Qui Parle County), 59 mph in Canby (Yellow Medicine County), 60 mph in Appleton (Swift County), 68 mph four miles southwest of Belle Plaine (Scott County), 58 mph seven miles south of Starbuck (Pope County), 63 mph in Mankato (Blue Earth County), and 65 mph two miles WSW of New Prague (Le Sueur County). Scattered reports of trees, large branches, and power lines down throughout the region were reported. Additionally, strong winds ripped the roof off a repair shop in St. Clair.  Shoreland Golf Course in St. Peter had so many downed trees they were forced to close for the day; the course superintendant said it was some of the worst damage he had ever seen. According to the Pope County Sheriff's office, an evergreen tree was knocked down over wires in Starbuck, a power pole was down on trees in Lowry, a shed was blown down in Cyrus, a power pole was toppled in Glenwood, and wires were down in Starbuck. According to the Mankato Free Press newspaper, several power lines blew over, and fallen trees blocked roads in the city of Mankato. Siding was ripped off a home in North Mankato. The State Patrol confirmed a recreational vehicle was overturned on Highway 22 about 5 miles north of Mankato. In New Prague, high winds toppled several trees and tree limbs, along with a power line, which affected customers northeast of the city.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very deep low pressure system trekked across northern Minnesota during the course of the 7th. A strong cold front attached to this low pressure area also swept through most of the state during this time frame.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three to four inch branches felled.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak area of surface low pressure was located near Winnipeg, Manitoba, Canada, while a cold front extended nearly straight to the west (into southeast Saskatchewan). Outflow from storms over southern Canada caused other storms to form by mid evening in northeast North Dakota, which then moved into northwest Minnesota just before midnight. These storms weakened and dissipated as they passed the Bemidji (MN) area a few hours after midnight. A weak upper level disturbance that lingered over northwest Minnesota helped one last thunderstorm cell develop in Roseau County around sunrise.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was uprooted near county road 22.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail caused severe damage to soybean fields. The amount of crop damage was not known.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air moved into west central Minnesota and remained for nearly a week. High temperatures from April 3rd to April 9th were mostly in the 20s to around 30 degrees with lows in the single digits and teens. The high temperatures were from 20 to 30 degrees below normal and lows were from 10 to 25 degrees below normal. Some record lows and many record low maximum temperatures were set throughout the period. The early spring cold had an affect on the residents, especially the farmers and ranchers, of west central Minnesota. Also, many robins died from the cold and lack of food.

Thunderstorms developed just before midnight on April 22nd. Hail up to quarter size was reported in and around the Grand Rapids area, including Coleraine. Penny sized hail was reported in southern Aitkin County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail fell for ten minutes.

A powerful low pressure area moved through central Wisconsin in early April, spreading heavy snow to much of northeastern Minnesota. The snow began during the early morning hours of the 3rd, and ended by late that evening. The heaviest snow fell across the north shore of Lake Superior through northern Pine County. A foot or more was recorded in these areas, with Silver Bay reporting 13.3 inches. 12.1 inches fell at the Duluth NWS, which was not only a record for the date, but also a record for any day in the month of April for the Port City. Lesser amounts of 6 to 10 inches fell in the interior Arrowhead region, including the Iron Range, as well as the Cass and Brainerd Lakes areas. In addition to the heavy snow, strong winds of over 20 mph created blowing and drifting conditions.

A sharp frontal boundary along with an upper level disturbance brought an unusual heavy snow event to west central Minnesota. Roads became snow covered or slushy making travel very difficult. Schools and events were either cancelled or postponed. Snowfall amounts included, 6 inches at Browns Valley, 7 inches at Ortonville, and 8 inches at Wheaton.

As a cold front moved into southeast Minnesota during the afternoon of June 7, it triggered a cluster of severe thunderstorms. Most storm damage was in areas south of a line from Winona to Preston. Law enforcement officials, amateur radio operators and storm spotters reported wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph, which knocked down trees. A shed and garage were also damaged near Rushford (Fillmore County).

An upper jet over the northern plains helped to enhance snow development with this system. At the surface, a weak area of low pressure tracked from north central Nebraska into southern Wisconsin, while an inverted trough extended up to the north. This brought about a 12 hour period of heavy snow, followed by gusty north northwest winds (on the 3rd). Numerous schools and activities closed across the area on Tuesday (3rd). Many vehicles were reported in the ditch, especially along Interstate 94 between Moorhead and Fergus Falls. 10 inches of snow was reported at Norcross, 9 inches near Battle Lake, and 7 inches at Ponsford, Wadena, and Wendell.

Drought conditions that began last summer continued over much of northeastern Minnesota. Extreme(D3) drought conditions persisted from points just north of the Iron Range, to the Canadian border, including much of the interior Arrowhead of Minnesota. Severe(D2) drought existed in an area from Lake Winnibigoshish, to just north of Duluth, to much of the north shore of Lake Superior.

A tight surface pressure gradient set up across eastern North Dakota and portions of the northwest quarter of Minnesota, between an area of surface low pressure over west central South Dakota and a high north of the Great Lakes. Evening upper air observations on the 5th and morning upper air observations on the 6th measured 60 to 70 mph wind speeds just off the surface. Some of these 60 to 70 mph wind speeds were pushed down to the surface across southeast North Dakota and portions of west central Minnesota, causing scattered tree and property damage. Rain showers helped to mix the stronger winds down to the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A MNDOT RWIS sensor 1 mile northwest of Rothsay measured 62 mph wind gusts between 747 and 752 am CST. In Foxhome, a 50 foot tall tree was uprooted and roof damage was reported around 750 am CST.

In Vergas, trees and powerlines were blown down around 750 am CST. Trees, powerlines, and a building under construction were also toppled in Erhard around 750 am CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through northeast Minnesota during the afternoon hours of July 26. A line of thunderstorms developed and became severe, producing damaging winds and some hail. Tree limbs were downed in the Duluth area as the storms passed through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limbs were downed and at least one vehicle was severely damaged at 59th Avenue East and London Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong and gusty winds developed across northeast Minnesota as an intense low pressure system pushed east of the region. Winds were generally sustained between 25 and 35 mph, but averaged close to 40 mph in Duluth and nearby locations. Wind gusts over 50 mph were common throughout the area with a gust to 60 mph at Duluth International Airport. Numerous trees and limbs were downed and some fell onto power lines, causing widespread power outages. About 200,000 homes were without power for more than 24 hours.

Extreme (D3) drought conditions returned by month's end to much of the Minnesota Arrowhead and to the southern portions of Cass, Crow Wing, and Aitkin counties. These areas, along with portions of southwest and central Minnesota, were declared federal disaster areas. Several locations reported record-setting drought conditions, including the Superior National Forest.||Rainfall for the month generally ranged from 1 to 3 inches below normal across northeast Minnesota. Just 0.17 inches of rain fell at Hibbing, the lowest ever observed in August. The water level on Lake Superior fell to a record low for August, surpassing the previous record low set in 1926. River and stream flows were also well below normal. Burning bans and restrictions were implemented across most of region as soil moisture levels remained extremely low, resulting in a high fire threat.

In addition, wind gusts of 60 to 65 mph were common, with a gust of 74 mph recorded at the Rochester International Airport.

A tight surface pressure gradient set up across eastern North Dakota and portions of the northwest quarter of Minnesota, between an area of surface low pressure over west central South Dakota and a high north of the Great Lakes. Evening upper air observations on the 5th and morning upper air observations on the 6th measured 60 to 70 mph wind speeds just off the surface. Some of these 60 to 70 mph wind speeds were pushed down to the surface across southeast North Dakota and portions of west central Minnesota, causing scattered tree and property damage. Rain showers helped to mix the stronger winds down to the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind gust was measured by an AWOS at the Detroit Lakes airport at 815 am CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms dropped between 3 and 4 inches of rain in a short duration over southern Wilkin County. An observer 1 mile west and 0.5 miles south of Tenney reported 3.4 inches of rain. The Rabbit River at Campbell showed a fairly quick rise too, starting at about 5.15 feet on the morning of the 5th and rising to about 9.15 feet by the afternoon of the 6th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several rural and farm access roads were flooded by the heavy rain. 3 to 4 inches of rain fell in a short amount of time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Frontal Boundary Induced Severe Weather Episode.
Frontal boundary induced severe weather episode.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS damage survey confirmed a weak tornado. It mostly crossed open fields, but did knock down several dozen trees, and caused minor structural damage at two farms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across North Dakota on the 26th, with a hot and humid airmass in place ahead of the front. At 6 pm CST, a surface low was located over southern Manitoba (Canada), just north of Pembina, ND. The cold front stretched to the southwest, back toward Devils Lake (ND) and Bismarck (ND). Ahead of the cold front, temperatures ranged in the low to mid 80s with dew points around 70F. Behind the front, temperatures did not cool significantly, but dew points dropped to the mid 40s. Thunderstorms began to form along and ahead of the cold front by early evening (from northeast ND into south central ND), eventually crossing into northwest Minnesota several hours later. Eight distinct tornadoes occurred over northeast North Dakota and northwest Minnesota during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Powerlines and trees were blown down, blocking highway 1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved slowly into southeast Minnesota during the late evening hours on September 30th and the early morning hours of October 1st. Thunderstorms along and ahead of the cold front produced excessive rainfall amounts of up to 3 inches in two hours. This caused flooding in northern Dodge County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flooding along the Middle Fork of the Zumbro River causing it to exceed 100 yards wide at some points.  A bridge along County Road 7 caused the river to dam which also led to more flooding.  Water was 2 to 3 feet deep running through fields and many roads were impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm frontal boundary near extreme South Central Minnesota produced some sporadic flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported significant street flooding in the city.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifting northward into Iowa triggered round after round of thunderstorms with excessive rainfall across southeast Minnesota during the evening and overnight hours of August 18-19. This was after rain had fallen earlier in the day.  Total rainfall amounts of 10 to 15 inches were common, which produced widespread and significant flash flooding. An official rainfall total of 15.1 inches near Hokah (Houston County) established an all-time 24 hour rainfall record for the state of Minnesota. Other rainfall totals, which were unofficial readings, included 17.00 and 14.00 inches respectively in Witoka and Utica (Winona County). Numerous roads and bridges were washed out, houses collapsed due to mudslides and hundreds of homes had flooded basements. Law enforcement and fire department officials reported rescuing people from their vehicles, as well as from house tops. There were seven fatalities reported, five in Winona County and two in Houston County. Damage attributed to the heavy rainfall and subsequent flooding was estimated to be in the millions of dollars. Fillmore, Houston, Olmsted, Wabasha and Winona counties were declared federal disaster areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding quickly hit area creeks, even into the Millville, Plainview, and Zumbro Falls area. Several roads were closed as flood waters peaked overnight including damage to some businesses and homes. A few mudslides hit County Roads 2 and 11, among other areas, including a huge boulder that washed on to a road at the start of the flooding. At least 44 families were impacted in Elgin with lower numbers in other communities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the first two weeks of September, extreme (D3) drought conditions were reported across portions of Saint Louis, Lake, Cook, Cass, Crow Wing, and Aitkin counties. After beneficial rains during the middle and latter part of the month, these areas were upgraded to severe (D2) drought status, while the remainder of northeast Minnesota reported abnormally dry (D0) to moderate (D1) drought conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weather disturbance combined with deep moisture and instability to produce severe thunderstorms with damaging winds and large hail over portions of northeast Minnesota from the afternoon hours of September 20 through midday September 21. ||One storm over Pine County brought strong straight line winds that downed numerous trees and braches. Some minor structural damage was observed in Pine City and along County Road 7 just west of town. There was also minor damage to fences, outbuildings, and two baseball fields at Pine City High School.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter-sized hail.

In addition to Rush Creek, every other creek that leads into Rushford had tremendous accumulation that led to flood waters raging into town. The water backed up against the levees normally used to keep flood waters out from the nearby Root River and ended up flooding most of the town, including the entire downtown area.||In Rushford specifically, two thirds of the town was under water at some point. About 300 people had to be rescued. 490 of the 766 homes were flooded or had damage (79 were declared structurally unsound). 58 of the 70 businesses in town were damaged impacting nearly 500 of the 600 jobs in town. A total of 4 churches and 40 apartment buildings were also impacted.||Damage was widespread across the county, especially the northeast parts. Infrastructure was hit hard with numerous road closures and bridge damage. Numerous mud slides were reported. At least 704 homes applied for FEMA assistance.

About 500 campers were evacuated that evening, but about a dozen campers were stranded at a primitive campsite overnight. They were rescued the following morning. The state park lost 3 bridges and there was extreme damage to campgrounds, rest rooms, a group dining hall, and the septic and water systems totaling at least one million dollars. The flood system installed at the park did activate that evening.||Many area creeks and trout streams were heavily damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme (D3) drought conditions returned by month's end to much of the Minnesota Arrowhead and to the southern portions of Cass, Crow Wing, and Aitkin counties. These areas, along with portions of southwest and central Minnesota, were declared federal disaster areas. Several locations reported record-setting drought conditions, including the Superior National Forest.||Rainfall for the month generally ranged from 1 to 3 inches below normal across northeast Minnesota. Just 0.17 inches of rain fell at Hibbing, the lowest ever observed in August. The water level on Lake Superior fell to a record low for August, surpassing the previous record low set in 1926. River and stream flows were also well below normal. Burning bans and restrictions were implemented across most of region as soil moisture levels remained extremely low, resulting in a high fire threat.

A cold front moved across North Dakota on the 26th, with a hot and humid airmass in place ahead of the front. At 6 pm CST, a surface low was located over southern Manitoba (Canada), just north of Pembina, ND. The cold front stretched to the southwest, back toward Devils Lake (ND) and Bismarck (ND). Ahead of the cold front, temperatures ranged in the low to mid 80s with dew points around 70F. Behind the front, temperatures did not cool significantly, but dew points dropped to the mid 40s. Thunderstorms began to form along and ahead of the cold front by early evening (from northeast ND into south central ND), eventually crossing into northwest Minnesota several hours later. Eight distinct tornadoes occurred over northeast North Dakota and northwest Minnesota during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck the bell tower of a church in Bejou, igniting a fire in the tower. The fire spread throughout the building, causing a total loss. The building had been recently renovated.

The tornado destroyed a steel pole shed and a wooden quonset at one farmstead with significant tree damage at other locations nearby. Peak winds were estimated at 120 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Twelve thousand acres of crop damage reported just north and east of the city, as reported by the Le Sueur Free Press. The County Emergency Manager reported most of the damage occurred in Tyron and Lexington townships. There was 100 percent loss to much of the corn, soybeans and small grain crops according to University of Minnesota Extension Agent Dave Pfarr. Reports of tennis ball (2.5 inch) and baseball size hail (2.75 inch) were described in the newspaper, but time and location were not given.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm frontal boundary induced severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large branches and power lines down near Lake Harriet and Lake Calhoun. Waves reached two feet, where sailing schools were being held. Five sailboats were tipped, tossing 16 children and adults into the two lakes. Nobody was injured. All were wearing life jackets. Most swam back to shore, while others were picked up by other boaters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front was the catalyst for a large severe weather event across much of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A ten mile long and two mile wide swath of hail caused significant crop damage, broke windows, and dented siding and vehicles.  At Mud Lake, 3 miles southeast of French Lake, the hail got progressively larger, reaching golf ball size just before it ended.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm frontal boundary induced severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain moved across a portion of St. Paul. The radar estimate depicted one inch or less of rain in an hour. Five workers successfully evacuated the sewer pipes 150 feet below ground, but two men were unable to reach the exit to the street, and died when they were swept through the tunnel system.  Their bodies were found in the Mississippi River close to the exit point of the sewer system.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: By noon CST on the 27th, a weak area of surface low pressure was located over southeast South Dakota, with a stationary front extending to its north-northeast (along a Lidgerwood, ND, to Waskish, MN, line). Thunderstorms initially formed along a line from south of Bismarck (ND) to near Bemidji (MN), and across the tri-state boundary of ND, SD, and MN. The event had deep moisture to tap into, with precipitable water values around 1.5 inches. By early evening, the storms had shifted off toward Brainerd and Duluth.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The large hail damaged vehicles, roofs, gutters, and skylights.

This episode of storms dealt with 4 distinct groupings. The first group of storms began near Nashua in southeast Wilkin County (MN) and tracked to the northeast. The second began near Lake Park in western Becker County (MN) and tracked to the east-northeast. The third began near Enderlin in northern Ransom County (ND) and tracked to the east-northeast. And finally, the fourth group of storms formed near Goodridge in northeast Pennington County (MN) and tracked to the east-northeast. These storms appeared to be elevated, forming near a tight 850 mb moisture gradient and a 35 to 45 knot low level jet. This event also had upper level jet support.

Following several rounds of beneficial rainfall, most areas were no longer experiencing drought conditions by mid-month, although moderate (D1) drought persisted across southern Cass County.

Dry conditions continued over portions of west central Minnesota throughout the month of September. Wadena and eastern Otter Tail Counties remained in a D2 (severe) drought designation the entire month. The U.S. Drought Monitor even included a small strip of southern Wadena and eastern Otter Tail Counties in a D3 (extreme) drought designation from September 11th through the end of the month. Grant County also started the month in a D2 (severe) drought designation and remained there until September 25th.

A warm front lifting northward into Iowa triggered round after round of thunderstorms with excessive rainfall across southeast Minnesota during the evening and overnight hours of August 18-19. This was after rain had fallen earlier in the day.  Total rainfall amounts of 10 to 15 inches were common, which produced widespread and significant flash flooding. An official rainfall total of 15.1 inches near Hokah (Houston County) established an all-time 24 hour rainfall record for the state of Minnesota. Other rainfall totals, which were unofficial readings, included 17.00 and 14.00 inches respectively in Witoka and Utica (Winona County). Numerous roads and bridges were washed out, houses collapsed due to mudslides and hundreds of homes had flooded basements. Law enforcement and fire department officials reported rescuing people from their vehicles, as well as from house tops. There were seven fatalities reported, five in Winona County and two in Houston County. Damage attributed to the heavy rainfall and subsequent flooding was estimated to be in the millions of dollars. Fillmore, Houston, Olmsted, Wabasha and Winona counties were declared federal disaster areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At least 50 mudslides were reported across the county with four state highways and eight county roads closed. At one point 20 roads were closed across the county. Some homes collapsed or were carried away by mudslides. Numerous railroad tracks and bridges washed out. There were numerous reports of injuries. Hardest hit communities included Houston, Hokah, La Crescent and Brownsville. A 25 foot earthen dam near Hokah, which was built in 1921, collapsed. This caused significant damage to a portion of the town, including the main park and an automobile dealership. First calls of flooding began around 9:30 p.m. Saturday August 18 to dispatch center, with flooding problems becoming exceedingly worse afterwards.||4,200 homes were reportedly damaged or destroyed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The large hail left small 3 inch craters on lawns. Many vehicles and homes were damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front triggered a line of thunderstorms during the evening hours on September 24th. An isolated severe storm produced wind gusts up to 60 mph in Dodge County, MN but there were numerous other reports of 45 to 52 mph wind gusts across southeast Minnesota. The public reported a couple of out-buildings and some trees blown down on a farm, as well as the roof blown off a barn.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two out-buildings and some trees were blown down and the roof of a barn was blown off. A large 10 x 10 foot dumpster was moved 50 feet.

The public reported hail sizes ranging from penny near Bear Valley to quarters near Theilman.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were blown down.

A moist and unstable air mass ahead of an approaching low pressure system produced thunderstorms and torrential rainfall that resulted in localized flooding in portions of Itasca County during the evening of September 18.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Itasca County Sheriff's Office repoorted U.S. Highway 169 under 4 to 7 inches of water in Taconite.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weather disturbance combined with deep moisture and instability to produce severe thunderstorms with damaging winds and large hail over portions of northeast Minnesota from the afternoon hours of September 20 through midday September 21. ||One storm over Pine County brought strong straight line winds that downed numerous trees and braches. Some minor structural damage was observed in Pine City and along County Road 7 just west of town. There was also minor damage to fences, outbuildings, and two baseball fields at Pine City High School.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The St. Louis County Sheriff's Office reported that winds up to 60 mph caused damage in French Township around Side Lake, with downed trees and power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm frontal boundary induced severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down on power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small cluster of thunderstorms near International Falls converged into a lone supercell over Koochiching County. The storm moved south into Itasca County, producing damaging straight line winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large basswood tree with a trunk diamter of twenty inches was blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced large hail over far southwestern Minnesota in the Jasper area during the early evening of May 8th. The hail fell near the Pipestone and Rock County line. A few hailstones up to golf ball size were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail was mostly penny size with a few golf ball size stones.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A front draped from northwest Minnesota to a low in the southern plains resulted in scattered showers and thunderstorms, with some of these becoming severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down. Gravity boxes rolled across yard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across southeast Minnesota during the evening hours of July 26th. This allowed a line of severe thunderstorms to track across parts of southeast Minnesota. The line of severe thunderstorms produced wind gusts of around 60 mph, blew off silo top and caused damage to trees.

A cold front moved across southeast Minnesota during the evening hours of July 26th. This allowed a line of severe thunderstorms to track across parts of southeast Minnesota. The line of severe thunderstorms produced wind gusts of around 60 mph, blew off silo top and caused damage to trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A silo top was blown off from estimated wind gusts of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across North Dakota on the 26th, with a hot and humid airmass in place ahead of the front. At 6 pm CST, a surface low was located over southern Manitoba (Canada), just north of Pembina, ND. The cold front stretched to the southwest, back toward Devils Lake (ND) and Bismarck (ND). Ahead of the cold front, temperatures ranged in the low to mid 80s with dew points around 70F. Behind the front, temperatures did not cool significantly, but dew points dropped to the mid 40s. Thunderstorms began to form along and ahead of the cold front by early evening (from northeast ND into south central ND), eventually crossing into northwest Minnesota several hours later. Eight distinct tornadoes occurred over northeast North Dakota and northwest Minnesota during this event.
Warm Frontal Boundary Induced Event from early afternoon on the 10th to early afternoon on the 11th.

Late in the afternoon of the 9th, a cold front was located just to the north of the Canadian border (across southern Saskatchewan, southern Manitoba, and southwestern Ontario). Temperatures at 500 millibars were quite cold. As temperatures warmed at the surface and an upper level disturbance moved through, thunderstorms were able to form and intensify enough to produce hail.

Around noon CST, a weak surface trough was located from Mahnomen County back into southeast North Dakota. Over the next several hours, the trough did not move much. By early afternoon, a weak surface low formed over central Minnesota, with a cold front extending back into southwest MN. A few thunderstorms flared up in the early afternoon near the trough over Mahnomen and Otter Tail Counties, but these storms did not last long.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain and wind also accompanied the hail. This knocked down some smaller tree branches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Around 3 pm CST, a fairly strong cold front was located along a line from Devils Lake, ND, to Hallock, MN. Throughout the evening, this cold front slowly sagged southward. By 6 pm CST, the front had reached a line from New Rockford, ND, to Roseau, MN. At this time, Jamestown, ND, reported a temperature of 86 degrees, while Devils Lake had dropped to 66 degrees. By midnight, the front was located along a line from Bemidji (MN) to Fargo (ND) to 20 miles south of Jamestown (ND). Behind the front, the temperature dropped to 39 degrees in Devils Lake a couple hours after midnight. Large hail, tornadoes, and flash flooding occurred during this event.

On the morning of the 13th, one large supercell tracked across southwest Benson County (ND) and western Eddy County (ND). This storm tracked southeast toward northern Barnes County (ND), then weakened as it moved into Cass County (ND). A second large supercell then developed in Towner County (ND) and tracked toward Thompson, ND. As the second storm moved into southeast Grand Forks County (ND), additional storms developed to the east along and just south of the U.S. Highway 2 corridor in Minnesota. As the second supercell moved across eastern Ramsey and northern Nelson Counties (in ND), it displayed a classic hook echo on radar.
Warm frontal boundary induced severe weather.
A cold frontal boundary was the catalyst for a string of severe thunderstorms across portions of Central Minnesota.
Warm frontal boundary induced severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hailed near County Roads 47 and 28.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm frontal boundary over the region was the impetus for scattered showers and thunderstorms. Some of these reached severe levels.
Warm frontal boundary induced severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated wind speeds of 50 mph at 845pm LST. No damage reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm frontal boundary induced severe weather.
Thunderstorms produced marginally large hail in Rock and Nobles counties in far southwest Minnesota near and shortly after midnight on the night of October 5th/6th.

Warm temperatures just above the surface layer kept this thunderstorm event restricted to the area right along the border between northwest Minnesota and Canada. Early in the evening of the 24th, a cold front was located from Hallock, Minnesota, to near Bismarck, North Dakota. Over the next couple of hours, the front sagged a bit south, toward a Baudette, MN, to Grafton, ND, line. Thunderstorms initially developed over Kittson and Roseau Counties, but moved into southern Manitoba and southwest Ontario, Canada.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief touchdown was reported near the Timberline Trailer Court on Minnesota Highway 11. A few large tree branches were broken off. The damage indicated peak winds around 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm frontal boundary induced severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail occurred at I-494 and Tamarack Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: By early evening, a weak surface low pressure area was located between Bismarck and Jamestown (ND), with a warm front arching northeast to Grafton (ND) and then toward the Upper and Lower Red Lakes in Minnesota. Thunderstorms produced some hail and strong winds north of the surface warm front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A possible microburst occurred at this location. Small trees and a flag pole were blown down and a few shingles were torn off a roof. A camper on jacks was tilted over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across North Dakota on the 26th, with a hot and humid airmass in place ahead of the front. At 6 pm CST, a surface low was located over southern Manitoba (Canada), just north of Pembina, ND. The cold front stretched to the southwest, back toward Devils Lake (ND) and Bismarck (ND). Ahead of the cold front, temperatures ranged in the low to mid 80s with dew points around 70F. Behind the front, temperatures did not cool significantly, but dew points dropped to the mid 40s. Thunderstorms began to form along and ahead of the cold front by early evening (from northeast ND into south central ND), eventually crossing into northwest Minnesota several hours later. Eight distinct tornadoes occurred over northeast North Dakota and northwest Minnesota during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down in the city of Thief River Falls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a cold front moved into southeast Minnesota during the afternoon of June 7, it triggered a cluster of severe thunderstorms. Most storm damage was in areas south of a line from Winona to Preston. Law enforcement officials, amateur radio operators and storm spotters reported wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph, which knocked down trees. A shed and garage were also damaged near Rushford (Fillmore County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A shed was demolished from strong thunderstorm winds.  Debris from the shed was blown into nearby fields.  A garage was also pushed free of a nearby home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front and unstable air mass combined to produce severe thunderstorms across southern Cass, Crow Wing, and Pine counties during the evening hours of August 13. A fast-moving severe thunderstorm in the Brainerd area brought large hail and winds up to 60 mph that downed hundreds of trees and damaged homes. Several businesses reported roof damage and a two-story structure at a local speedway collapsed. Docks along several lakes in Crow Wing County also reported damage. At least 7,700 customers lost power in the Brainerd Lakes region, according to Crow Wing Power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail covered the ground.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail covered the ground. A six-inch diameter oak tree was blown down by strong thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across southeast Minnesota late in the evening of August 11, triggering a line of severe thunderstorms. Hail up to the size of golf balls was reported by storm spotters and law enforcement officials. In addition, wind gusts of 60 to 65 mph were common, with a gust of 74 mph recorded at the Rochester International Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 74 miles per hour was measured at the Rochester International Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced heavy rain, which caused flash flooding, in Nobles and Jackson Counties in southwest Minnesota during the morning of August 20th. The flash flooding affected roads in both rural and urban areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall of 3 to 5 inches in a short time caused flash flooding of streets in the towns of Bigelow and Worthington, as well as flooding of rural roads and fields. Streets in downtown Worthington were especially affected.
